1.0 PURPOSE & SCOPE

1.1 MH Construction, Inc. is committed to providing a safe and healthful work environment for all employees, subcontractors, and visitors on every project.

1.2 This Safety & Health Program applies to all MHC operations, including commercial construction, remodel, restoration, and government contracts in Washington State.

1.3 This program is aligned with the AGC Construction Safety Excellence Awards (CSEA) framework, OSHA 29 CFR 1926, WAC 296-155 (WISHA), and Travelers Risk Control best practices.

2.0 PROGRAM FRAMEWORK

The MHC Safety Program is organized into 8 chapters and 50 MISH sections, covering all aspects of construction safety from program governance to emergency response.

Chapter

Sections

Focus Area

1

MISH 01–05

Program Administration & Governance

2

MISH 06–12

Personnel Management & Workforce Safety

3

MISH 13–20

Hazard Identification & Control

4

High-Risk Operations

5

MISH 33–42

Equipment & Vehicle Safety

6

MISH 43–45

Environmental & Health Programs

7

MISH 46–47

Subcontractor & Contract Management

8

MISH 48–50

Emergency Response & Incident Management

3.0 MANAGEMENT COMMITMENT

3.1 Senior management of MH Construction, Inc. is committed to providing the resources necessary to implement and maintain this safety program.

3.2 The President and Safety Officer are jointly responsible for program oversight, annual review, and ensuring all sections remain current with OSHA, WISHA, AGC, and Travelers requirements.

3.3 This program is reviewed annually and updated following any significant incident, regulatory change, or Travelers Risk Control consultation.

MISH 02 — Injury-Free Workplace Commitment

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Mh Construction Industrial Safety And Health Program

Title

Injury Free Workplace Plan

Number

Mish 1

Revision

1

Approved By

Jeremy Thamert 03/01/2025

Name Date

Effective Date

3/1/25

Page

1 of 4

GENERAL. The MH Construction Inc. (MH Construction) Safety Process is solidly based on the INJURY FREE WORKPLACE philosophy, through High Performance Safety. The key elements of this process consist of the following:

  • Leadership

Establishing Safety as a Value rather than a priority.

  • Behavior

Acceptance and commitment to safety by Choice rather than compliance.

  • Environment

Maintaining the culture of the workplace at a High Moral.

MISSION. MH Construction is committed to embracing this INJURY FREE WORKPLACE and NON-PERMITTED HAZARDOUS MATERIAL RELEASE FREE philosophy with the sole intent of eliminating incidents and resulting injuries and non-permitted discharges or releases with respect to the environment on the project site. Summarized:

“We cannot, and will not, hurt our people and/or the environment”

PLAN ELEMENTS. To attain an Injury Free Workplace and a higher state of continuous improvement in the above mission, the following programs, policies and procedures have been developed, adopted and implemented by MH Construction on all its projects.

1. Orientation. Orientation will include MH Construction’s Accident Prevention Program and thorough and detailed presentation of Safety material, procedures and practices to all new employees, prior to reporting for work, with added emphasis on MH Construction’s INJURY FREE WORKPLACE philosophy.

2. Drug Free Workplace. Employees are required to follow MH Construction’s Drug Free Workplace program which requires pre-employment, random, post accident/incident and for cause testing.

3. Discipline Policy. First warning regarding the need for safe work performance is conveyed to the worker during orientation. A violation of safe practices and procedures may result in suspension or termination of the employee.

  • First Violation – Written Warning
  • Second Violation – Four Day Suspension/Without Pay
  • Third Violation - Termination

NOTE: MH Construction reserves the right to execute immediate termination based on the hazard associated with the behavior.

4. Subcontractor Pre-Qualification. Pre-bid, post-bid, and other preliminary meetings will provide detailed safety direction and guidance to the Subcontractor. The Subcontractor will acknowledge and commit to MH Construction’s and any owners/general contractors safety requirements before commencing work. If subcontractors Experience modification rate and or incident rates are above general contractors or owners criteria for a safe contractor the subcontractor shall submit a corrective action plan that is acceptable to MH Construction Safety and general contractor or owner’s Safety department prior to awarding a subcontract.

5. Safety Representative. MH Construction will designate a Site Safety Representative on all projects. The position may be filled by a qualified worker or foreman.

6. Quality of Life. Adequate facilities are provided to enhance the worker quality of life and increase morale. Toilet facilities, lunchrooms, parking, lighting, access ways, and other conveniences will be considered.

7. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE). Head protection (hard hat), eye protection (safety glasses), hand protection (gloves), foot protection (sturdy, leather work boots), full-length pants and shirts with sleeves (minimum 4” below the shoulder) are to be worn when the hazard exists. Hearing protection and high visibility vests will be worn as directed by the Project Superintendent.

8. Training Requirements. Employees are required to participate in safety training as outlined in the contract and MH Construction’s Industrial Safety and Health Manual, to include but not limited to, fall protection, scaffolding, trenching and excavation, equipment operation, CPR/First Aid, and JSA’s, JHA’s.

9. Meeting Requirements. Safety centered meetings are required on a weekly basis. Examples are Job Hazard Analysis, Pre-Task Planning, and Weekly Safety Meetings.

10. Safety Planning Process. Safety planning begins with the 3-week look-ahead to define the scope of work and establish safety and health requirements, it’s conducted at three levels: Strategic (MH Construction), Operational (Subcontractor/MH Construction), and Action (Crew).

  • Strategic – Phase Safety Plan (PSP). MH Construction will initiate the safety planning process based on the scheduled phases of construction. Key activities, and training and operational requirements to perform work safely, are identified and referred to the appropriate Subcontractor for planning and action or (for self performed work- Superintendent).
  • Operational - Job Hazard Analysis (JHA). Superintendent/Subcontractor utilizes the JHA process to identify all hazards, safety requirements and controls related with all tasks performed in a given phase of construction. JHA’s are submitted and reviewed prior to beginning work.
  • Action - Pre-Task Plan (PTP). Crew completes a PTP daily for the specific tasks they will undertake that day, or when the scope of work changes, to ensure hazards are noted and safety measures are implemented.

11. Incident Reviews. All incidents, regardless of injury, are reviewed within 24-hours of occurrence.

  • Reviews are attended by MH Construction management, the employee(s) involved, the employee’s foreman and superintendent, and witnesses to the incident.
  • Sequence of events, contributing factors, root cause and corrective action are determined.
  • Lessons learned are published and discussed at safety meetings.

12. Safety Audits. Daily site inspections are conducted by the Key Supervisor and/or Safety Representatives, findings noted and corrective action planned.

13. Safety Self-Assessment (SSA). A Safety Self-Assessments will be conducted during the course of the project, by the Project Management Team.

14. SDS Approval. Hazardous chemicals/materials are required to have MH Construction approval prior to the on-site delivery and use of the product.

  • The Safety Data Sheet (SDS) is the basis for that approval.
  • SDS’s for each product will be submitted to MH Construction/General contractor or Owner for review and approval.
  • Hazardous chemical inventory and reporting usage shall meet the contract requirements.

15. Project Management Involvement. Managers, superintendents, and project support staff will become intricately familiar with project safety and field operations. To accomplish this, the following methods are suggested.

  • Safety Management By Walking Around (SMBWA). Involvement by becoming acquainted with the project and the workers on a personal and professional basis.
  • Meeting Attendance. Personal involvement at safety meetings
  • Follow-up. Addressing safety issues immediately upon knowledge and following through with corrective action.
  • Visible Leadership. Maximize team resources to effectively and efficiently lead the Site Safety Process. Planning, Organizing, Coordinating and Communicating are the principle vehicles to accomplish this.

16. Subcontractor flow down. Subcontracts to subcontractors shall have flow down requirements to ensure all subcontractors meet the minimum MH Construction’s safety requirement or Owners safety requirements.

17. Equipment Inspection. To ensure Zero non-permitted releases of hazardous chemicals to the environment all equipment will have a thorough documented inspection prior to being allowed on site. Any deficiency shall be corrected prior to mobilization. Daily inspection and monthly documented inspections shall be performed to ensure all items are working safely and will eliminate any spills. Manufactures recommendations shall be followed for schedule maintenance activities.

18. Accountability and Responsibility. The project manager has overall responsibility for implementation of the contract and MH Construction’s Accident Prevention Manual and is accountable for the safe performance of their projects. Superintendent/key supervisors are responsible and accountable to ensure an injury free work environment, compliance with applicable procedures, federal, and contract safety requirements. Workers are responsible to follow the work rules and good working practice.

19. Job Site –Good Order and Housekeeping Policy: It is the policy of the MH Construction group to maintain good project housekeeping, order and organization. We believe that while not every clean jobsite is safe, every safe job site is clean. Project Managers and site superintendents will ensure that housekeeping and cleanliness is maintained regularly on the project floor, trailers and storage facilities – including associated laydown areas. All areas will be maintained free of general trash and debris, including the work areas, office and break facilities. Work areas will be swept down daily, while all materials and equipment will be stored neatly, in an organized manner, thus enhancing jobsite safety, morale and productivity.

Training & Reference Resources

Travelers Risk Control: https://www.travelers.com/resources/business-topics/workplace-safety

Travelers Training Library: https://www.travelers.com/risk-control/safety-education-training

OSHA Reference: https://www.osha.gov/construction

OSHA Reference: https://www.osha.gov/laws-regs/regulations/standardnumber/1926

AGC Safety Resources: https://www.agc.org/learn/education-training/safety-health

WISHA / WA L&I: https://lni.wa.gov/safety-health/

NOTE: WISHA citations apply only where WAC 296-155 is MORE STRINGENT than OSHA 29 CFR 1926.

1.0 PURPOSE

This section defines the safety responsibilities of all personnel within MH Construction, Inc. Clear role definition is a foundational requirement of the AGC CSEA framework and OSHA 29 CFR 1926.20.

2.0 ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES

2.1 President / Owner

  • Establish and communicate the company safety policy.
  • Allocate resources for safety program implementation and training.
  • Review and approve all MISH program updates annually.
  • Sign all Revision/Approval blocks as the authorizing executive.
  • Participate in Travelers Risk Control consultations.

2.2 Safety Officer

  • Serve as the primary point of contact for all safety matters.
  • Conduct and document jobsite safety inspections.
  • Maintain all MISH documentation, training records, and incident reports.
  • Coordinate drug and alcohol testing per MISH 06.
  • Respond to Travelers Risk Control recommendations within required timeframes.
  • Maintain current OSHA 30-Hour Construction certification.
  • Coordinate with AGC safety resources and Travelers Risk Control Consultant.

2.3 Project Manager / Superintendent

  • Enforce all MISH program requirements on assigned projects.
  • Conduct daily pre-task safety briefings (toolbox talks).
  • Immediately report all incidents and near-misses to the Safety Officer.
  • Verify all subcontractors comply with MISH 46 requirements.
  • Maintain a current copy of the MISH binder on every active jobsite.

2.4 All Employees

  • Follow all MISH program requirements as a condition of employment.
  • Report unsafe conditions or near-misses immediately to supervision.
  • Participate in all required safety training and orientations.
  • Use required PPE at all times in designated areas.
  • Never operate equipment or vehicles while impaired.

2.5 Subcontractors

  • Comply with all MHC MISH program requirements while on MHC projects.
  • Provide proof of safety training and insurance per MISH 46 and MISH 47.
  • Report all incidents on MHC projects to the MHC Project Manager.

Training & Reference Resources

Travelers Risk Control: https://www.travelers.com/risk-control

Travelers Training Library: https://www.travelers.com/risk-control/safety-education-training

1.0 PURPOSE

To define the requirements, responsibilities, and system for orienting employees to the MH Construction Industrial Safety and Health (MISH) program, and instructing employees to the job-specific requirements. To ensure compliance with Occupational Safety and Health Administration/Washington Industrial Safety and Health Act (OSHA/WISHA) requirements.

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 MH Construction is responsible for the following:

2.1.1 Assisting contractors in identifying area-specific emergency response and notification requirements.

2.2 The Key Supervisor shall be responsible for the following:

2.2.1 Orienting their employees and lower-tier contractor employees to the MISH program and completing the Orientation Checklist (attachment MISH 4-A) prior to individual employees beginning work activities.

NOTE: Escorted personnel including vendors, engineers and infrequent visitors, can be exempt from the MISH program orientation provided they are escorted and kept out of hazardous areas.

2.2.2 Ensuring each employee and lower-tier employee has reviewed the applicable/required MISH procedures and signed the Orientation Signature Sheet (attachment MISH 4-B).

2.2.3 The checklist and signature sheet shall be available at the jobsite.

2.3 Employees shall be responsible for understanding and complying with the MISH program and job-specific requirements.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Not Applicable

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 Key Supervisors/Safety Representative shall orient employees to the MISH program as identified on the Orientation Checklist, and to procedures the Contractor identifies that are applicable to the project. These procedures must be checked on the Orientation Checklist.

4.2 Topics to be covered during the orientation (as a minimum) include:

 Employer rights and responsibilities

 Disciplinary procedures

 Alcohol and Drug Abuse policies

 Access to exposure monitoring data/records

 Location of JHAs and company safety program

 Emergency signals, response, and notification requirements for the area

 Location of fire extinguishers, pull box alarms, emergency evacuation routes, and first aid facilities

 How to report injuries

 How to report unsafe conditions

 The use and care of personal protective equipment

 Identification of hazards gases, chemicals and materials; instruction on the hazards of these products and their safe use

 Instruct employees regarding smoking in Owner’s facilities. Additionally, contractors are encouraged to establish designated smoking areas for their facilities. The designated smoking area provides a healthier workplace for the employee where “passive smoke” effects are minimized and the rights of smokers are also respected.

 Location of nearest Medical Aid Station

 Location of nearest telephone

4.3 The employees, after understanding the procedures identified in the Orientation checklist, sign the Orientation Signature Sheet.

Not Applicable

1.0 PURPOSE

To define the requirements for installing and maintaining safety bulletin board, and to ensure compliance with applicable state, federal and owner safety and health requirements.

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 The Key supervisor/Subcontractor is responsible for installing and maintaining a safety bulletin board at the location where the majority of employees report to work.

2.2 Mobile crews shall be advised to the location of the nearest bulletin board.

2.3 Employees shall be responsible for reviewing the bulletin board to keep informed of safety related information.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Not Applicable

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 Every job site with eight or more persons shall install and maintain a safety bulletin board where the majority of employees report to work.

4.2 Safety bulletin boards shall be sufficient size to display and post safety bulletins, newsletters, posters, accident statistics, and other safety educational material.

4.3 As a minimum, the safety bulletin board shall post the following:

  • DOE Occupational Safety and Health Poster DOE-F 5480.2 (DOE sites only)
  • DOE Occupational Safety and Health Complaint Form 5480.4 (DOE sites only)
  • Industrial Insurance Labor and Industries (Federal or State Specific)
  • Citations and Notices-as needed
  • OSHA 300 form during February 1st to March 1st.
  • Owner supplied safety bulletins, publications and posters.

4.4 Suggested items to be posted include the following:

  • Safety and Health posters
  • Minutes of safety meetings
  • Information on accidents
  • Lessons Learned

5.0 PROCEDURE

Not Applicable

6.0 RECORDS

Not Applicable

7.0 REFERENCES

29 CFR 1910 Occupational Safety and Health Standards (OSHA)

29 CFR 1926 Safety and Health Regulations for Construction (OSHA)

WAC 296-155 Safety Standards for Construction Work (WISHA)

MISH 06 — Drug & Alcohol Policy & Testing

Chapter 2: Personnel Management & Workforce Safety | 29 CFR 1926.20 | AGC: Program Management

DOCUMENT

3.0

Jeremy Thamert

President

Safety Officer

Travelers Risk Control Consultant

AGC Representative

Jeremy Thamert

Matt Ramsey

_______________________

_______________________

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

1.0 Policy Statement:

1.1 MH Construction Inc. (hereinafter referred to as “MH Construction” or the company) maintain a strong commitment to all the workers on its projects to:

▪ Provide a safe work environment

▪ Protect their health and safety

▪ Promote high standards of performance

1.2 Consistent with these commitments, we have established the following comprehensive Drug and Alcohol Policy. The primary objective of this policy is to promote and maintain a safe and healthy work environment that is free from the harmful effects of alcohol and drugs.

1.3 Drug and alcohol use is a national problem found in every part of society and spreading to every industry and occupation. Studies establish that drug and alcohol use alone each year costs the American economy billions of dollars in lost productivity, increased rates of employee absenteeism, poor performance, on-the-job injuries, higher medical costs, and rising thefts.

1.4 While we have no intention of interfering with the private lives of our workers, we expect all personnel to report to work “Fit for Duty”, which means in a condition to perform their duties in a safe, effective manner. An individual’s off-the-job, as well as on-the-job involvement with drugs and alcohol can have a significant negative impact on the workplace and can present a substantial risk to the individual who is using alcohol and drugs, to co-workers, and to others. MH Construction will not tolerate drug or alcohol use on or affecting its jobsites or property.

2.0 POLICY ADMINISTRATION:

2.1 To ensure thorough understanding and compliance with MH Construction’s above titled Drug and Alcohol Policy, all MH Construction employees, not covered under a bargaining union drug and alcohol program, will be required to read and agree to this policy as a condition of employment or access to any MH Construction projects or properties. Failure to comply with this requirement will make an applicant ineligible for access to MH Construction projects or properties.

2.2 Prohibited Substances, Articles and Conduct:

2.2.1 Alcohol: Alcohol is defined as any beverage or substance containing alcohol. The possession, transfer, offering, use or being under the influence of alcohol while on company property, premises or jobsites, or while on company time, or on company business, or in other circumstances that might adversely affect our operations, safety, or reputation is strictly prohibited. Prohibited conduct includes consumption or any alcohol on the job, or during business hours, or prior to reporting to work at any time, which affects the employee’s job performance.

2.2.2 Drugs: A drug is defined as any substance, which may impair mental or motor function including, but not limited to illegal drugs, controlled substances, designer drugs, synthetic drugs, look-alike drugs and, under circumstances described in this policy, prescription drugs. The possession, buying or selling, transfer, offering, use or being under the influence of any drug (except as authorized and prescribed by a physician and then only if reported to the Safety Director prior to beginning work) while on company property, premises or jobsites, or while on company time, or in other circumstances that might adversely affect our operations, safety, or reputation is strictly prohibited. Anyone testing positive for a drug, in both the basic and positive test verifications, will be considered under the influence of the drug. Prohibited conduct includes consumption of any amount of drugs on the job, or during business hours, or prior to reporting to work at any time, which causes a positive test result.

2.2.2.1 Prescription Drugs: A drug prescribed to an individual by a licensed physician for medical reasons. These must be taken within the limits of the prescribed instructions.

2.2.2.2 Illegal Drugs: Any drug that is not prescribed to that specific individual, to exclude over the counter drugs, or prescription drugs that have expired or not taken following the direction of the physician.

2.2.3 Contraband: Any items or actions considered contraband to an onsite worker as defined by state or federal laws for illegal drugs and associated items.

2.3 Testing of employees:

2.3.1 Employees will be required to undergo a body fluids test under the following circumstances:

2.3.1.1 Pre-employment: Prior to the start of, and as a condition of employment on an MH Construction contract or properties, prospective workers will be required to sign the specimen to test for the presence of prohibited substances. Presence of one or more prohibited substances or inconclusive test results will be cause for rejection of employment with MH Construction. Refusal to provide a specimen for the test will be considered voluntary withdrawal of the application for employment. The prospective employee will conduct the drug test prior to showing up at the job location. MH Construction will pay for the pre-employment drug test regardless of test results.

2.3.1.2 Random: MH Construction employees will be set into a random drug & alcohol testing pool. The rate will be equal to 30 percent of the group population for each 12 month period. Selection for testing will be performed by a business sensitive computerized system. Notification will be given to the employee by their responsible supervisor in a confidential manner, and that employee will then have 3 hours to provide a sample. If testing is not complete within the 3 hour time or the employee fails to show up to the testing location, the test shall be considered a positive.

2.3.1.3 Post Accident: Post accident/incident, or an “occurrence”, drug & alcohol testing will be performed on all involved personnel unless Supervision & or Safety determine otherwise. Escorting of the personnel to the collection facility will be arranged by supervision.

2.3.1.4 Reasonable Suspicion: When MH Construction supervision has reason to suspect employee drug use or alcohol intoxication, testing will be performed in accordance with the provisions of Section 2.4.

2.4 Testing procedures:

2.4.1 All body fluids tests will be performed in accordance with standards and requirements found in the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) Mandatory Guidelines. These guidelines provide direction for random selection of employees for testing; obtaining urine specimens for testing; specimen chain of custody forms and assurances; laboratory methodology including analysis sensitivity levels; and quality control.

Screening for alcohol will conform to Department of Transportation procedures. These procedures establish requirements for specimen collection, specimen analysis, quality assurance, invalid tests, refusal to test, uncompleted tests, privacy, and disclosure of alcohol testing information, and record keeping.

Body fluids tests will normally utilize only urine specimens. Tests which entail the withdrawal of blood will be exercised only in situations involving an injury accident where an employee is rendered unconscious and unable to provide a urine specimen.

2.4.2 Basic tests which give a positive indication of drug use above the thresholds outlined in ADDENDUM “A” will be confirmed by an alternate pre-approved method of testing as outlined in ADDENDUM “B”.

2.4.3 An employee suspected of being under the influence of a prohibited substance shall, for reasons of safety, be tested and suspended until test results have been reviewed by the appropriate agency. If a test proves negative, the employee will be reinstated with back pay.

2.4.4 Workers who test positive with a 0.02 Blood Alcohol Concentration (BAC) will be sent home the first infraction and suspended in accordance to 2.6 on the second infraction. Workers who test positive with a 0.04 BAC are subject to immediate suspension in accordance with 2.6.

2.5 Prescription drugs:

2.5.1 Employees using a prescription drug which may impair mental or motor functions shall inform the Safety Coordinator of such drug use. For the safety of all employees, the company may place persons using such drugs in a less hazardous job assignment, or place them on temporary medical leave until released as fit-for-duty by the prescribing physician. The company reserves the right to have its physician determine if a prescription drug produces hazardous effects, or to restrict the quantity the employee is allowed to bring to the workplace.

2.6 Disciplinary action:

2.6.1 Applicants testing positive/inconclusive will not be hired. Applicants may be eligible for re-hire in one year and after completing a drug and/or alcohol rehabilitation referral program and test negative (refer to 2.7) for all positive test. Employee’s testing that is inconclusive may be eligible for hire if split test comes back negative.

2.6.2 Workers found using or selling drugs on company premises, or company time, will be subject to immediate termination and are in-eligible for re-hire.

2.6.3 Workers found under the influence of alcohol, or testing positive for drugs in both the basic and positive test verification while operating any equipment on company premises, or while driving any company or subcontractor vehicle will be subject to immediate termination.

2.6.1 Workers who violate the Contraband Laws in any way will be subject to immediate termination.

2.6.2 Workers who violate this Drug and Alcohol Policy in any other way, including testing positive for drugs, in both the basic and positive test verification will be given the opportunity to select immediate termination or rehabilitation and Treatment (refer to 2.7).

2.6.2.1 Immediate Termination. The employee’s CONFIDENTIAL personnel file will be updated to include the nature and date of the violation. From that date on the individual will be permanently ineligible for employment with MH Construction unless the employee completes a drug/alcohol rehabilitation program and tests negative.

2.7 Rehabilitation and treatment:

2.7.1.1 Referral, counseling and rehabilitation. If the employee is eligible (and if the employee has not previously used it) the employee will be suspended without compensation during the time of counseling and treatment. Treatment will be as determined by an independent counselor. In all cases, counseling, treatment, and follow-up drug testing expenses will be the responsibility of the employee. When the counseling or rehabilitation has started and the employee completes a negative drug test, the employee may be eligible for employment with MH Construction.

2.7.2 The employee may be allowed to return to work with the company only after he/she agrees to and signs an “Agreement of Continuing Employment”. After the employee has returned to work he/she will be subject to follow-up drug (to be paid by the employee) tests, which may occur at any time during the mandatory one-year probationary period following reinstatement. A positive result from such a test, or any subsequent tests, will lead to immediate termination. The employee may then be permanently ineligible for employment with MH Construction. If required by the company, the employee may have to finish the prescribed rehabilitation program satisfactorily before the end of the one-year probationary period. Failure to complete the program, or unsatisfactory participation in this program, will result in immediate termination. Anyone refusing to cooperate with testing procedures will face the disciplinary alternative consistent with a positive test.

2.7.3 Individuals will be encouraged to seek help for a drug or alcohol problem before it deteriorates into a disciplinary matter.

2.7.4 If an individual voluntarily notifies his/her supervisor that he/she may have a substance abuse problem, the company will assist in locating suitable counseling and treatment services, and will counsel the individual regarding benefits available under their company or union insurance programs. If treatment necessitates a leave of absence, accrued vacation and sick leave time may be used (if applicable). All cost associated with treatment is the employee’s responsibility.

2.7.5 Individuals returning to work after such a leave of absence will be subject to drug tests at any time during a mandatory one-year probationary period. A positive test will result in immediate removal from the site and may make the individual permanently ineligible for employment within the company.

2.8 Confidentiality:

All actions taken under this policy will be CONFIDENTIAL within the employer and the union (if any) representing the employee.

3.0 SITUATIONS NOT COVERED BY THIS POLICY:

3.1 Situations may arise which are not specifically covered by this policy. For example, situations involving employees who have been arrested or convicted for off-the-job illegal drug use or activity. Such situations will be dealt with on a case-by-case basis taking into account such things as the nature of the problem, the employee’s overall employment record and job assignment, the potential impact on production, safety and customer and public perceptions or the company and it’s services. Corrective or disciplinary action will be determined by the MH Construction Safety Coordinator and their Division Manager.

4.0 ARREST OR CONVICTION UNDER CRIMINAL DRUG STATUTE:

4.1 Individuals must notify the MH Construction Safety Coordinator within five days of an arrest or conviction of a criminal drug statute. Failure to do so will result in immediate removal from the company.

5.0 RESERVATION OF RIGHTS:

5.1 The company reserves the right to revise this policy. Nothing in this policy alters an individual’s status. The company hopes each employment relationship will be a happy and enduring one. Nevertheless, workers remain free to resign their employment at any time for any or no reason. MH Construction’s “Reservation of Rights” is also subject to any express modification contained in any applicable collective bargaining agreement.

6.0 AMENDMENTS TO POLICY:

6.1 Amendments to this policy may be issued to comply with project Owner requirements, state or local laws, or federal contract requirements.

7.0 SUBSTANCE ABUSE TESTING

7.1 Prior to employment, personnel shall complete a quick or instant test. The test shall be completed prior to showing up at the job location. Inconclusive quick or instant test results will be cause for rejection of employment with MH Construction.

7.2 Drug and alcohol testing of individuals shall be required as soon as practical, subsequent to work-related accidents. A work-related accident is defined as an accident that could have resulted in an injury requiring treatment by a physician (OSHA recordable) or could have resulted in damage to property or equipment.

7.4 Random testing-employees will be notified of when and where to take the drug test. Testing shall be done during working hours and completed within 3 hours of employee notification. If testing is not complete within the 3 hour time, the test shall be considered a positive.

7.5 Reporting of test results shall be handled discretely between the medical facility and the MH Construction Safety Coordinator. Results shall be reported by the facility as follows:

7.5.1 NEGATIVE, the individual is within the limits prescribed in “ADDENDUM A”

7.5.2 POSITIVE, the individual is not within the limits prescribed in “ADDENDUM A”

7.5.3 INCONCLUSIVE, a non-negative test utilizing quick or instant testing.

7.1 If requested by the individual, the results will be provided directly to the individual.

7.2 MH Construction and the medical facility agree that customary patient privacy in taking the described test will be provided.

7.3 MH Construction, the medical facility and the testing laboratory agree that security of biological specimens is absolutely necessary. Any breach of this security will require an immediate retest.

7.4 In the event of positive test results on both the basic and positive verifications test, the employee may have, within (3) days, the same specimen retested at a different licensed laboratory. Retesting shall be performed at the individual’s expense. In the event the retested results are different, the negative results will be accepted.

7.5 MH Construction, the medical facility, and the testing laboratory agree that the results of the described tests are to be held in strictest CONFIDENCE between the MH Construction Safety Director and the medical facility. It is further agreed that the results of the above test will not be provided to anyone other than the Safety Director at MH Construction without the written consent of the employee.

Addendum “B”

Laboratory Procedures

EMIT METHOD INFORMATION (Basic Test)

The name EMIT stands for Enzyme-Multiplied Immunoassay Technique. This technique is used for the microanalysis of drugs in biological fluids. Urine is mixed with two reagents. Reagent One contains antibodies specific to the particular drug, the coenzyme nicontinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD) and the substrate G.6P (glucose 6 phosphate). Reagent Two contains a drug derivative labeled with ten enzymes G.6PDh. (glucose 6 phosphate dehydrogenate). Reagent one is added to the specimen and the antibody binds to any drug molecule in the patient specimen it recognizes. Reagent two is added next and the enzyme labeled drug combines with any remaining antibody; this binding decreases the enzyme label activity. The enzyme that remains unbound (therefore still active) relates directly to the concentration of drug in the specimen. The active enzyme converts NAD to NDAH, resulting in an absorbance change that is measured spectrophotometrically.

GAS CHROMATOGRAPHY-MASS SPECTROMETRY (Positive test verification):

GS/MS is used as a confirmation of positive EMIT screens in laboratories. It generally allows identification of the specific drug involved. It also provides the ability to quantitate the cannabinoid and urine alcohol level present.

GC/MS is an analytical technique which allows one to separate substances based on their differential migration rate through a gas chromatographic column and then, virtually unequivocally, identify them based on their mass spectrum and the migration time. The technique has been generally accepted as a reference method for legal applications. GC/MS does have limitations related to sensitivity, the range of masses measurable and the requirements for specimen volatility and the thermal stability. In general however, for drug analysis, GC/MS is the most specific method available.

The GC/MS test is performed with a Hewlett-Packard model, 5970-B, which uses electron bombardment to produce a “finger-print” mass spectrum of each analyte as it elutes from the capillary column in the gas chromatography. An on-board computer correlates the mass spectrum with a stored library of compounds and produces a report of the probable substances in the specimen.

Mh Construction

Applicant Consent To Drug And Alcohol Testing

I, ____________________________________________________________________, hereby consent and agree to give specimens of my body fluids* at a medical facility designated by MH Construction for transmittal and testing by an approved testing laboratory.

It is my understanding that the ultimate testing facility is licensed by the State, and body fluid* specimens will be tested to detect the presence of alcohol an/or other drugs in my body. All testing will be in accordance with standards currently disseminated and approved by the National Institute on Drug Abuse.

In addition to testing at the initial time of entry onto the job site, in the event I am directly involved in a work-related accident, or as part of on-site drug screening, or if there is reason to suspect my use of drugs or alcohol intoxication, I agree and consent to provide specimens of my body fluids* for testing to determine the presence of alcohol and/or drugs.

It is agreed that, upon request, I will be furnished results of tests performed on my body fluids* specimen by MH Construction, the employer, or the testing laboratory. The testing laboratory is only authorized to confirm to MH Construction and the employer whether test results are POSITIVE or NEGATIVE in accordance with predetermined threshold levels.

*Body fluids tests will normally utilize only urine specimens. Test, which entails the withdrawal of blood, will be exercised only in situations involving an injury accident where I am rendered unconscious and unable to provide a urine specimen, and I agree and consent to such a test under those circumstances.

I acknowledge that I have received, read and understand a copy of MH Construction’s Drug and Alcohol Policy. Furthermore, I understand that refusal to submit to the alcohol and drug screening test will constitute voluntary withdrawal of my application to enter the job site; if already on site, refusal to submit to such testing will result in exclusion from the job site and the presence of one or more of those prohibited drugs at or above the defined threshold levels will result in disciplinary action as outlined in the Drug and Alcohol Policy.

I, ______________________________________________________________________ understand and agree that my continued employment with MH Construction is conditioned upon my being fit for duty, and not working under the influence of alcohol or drugs at any time.

I hereby consent and agree to give specimens of my body fluids* at a medical facility designated by MH Construction for transmittal and testing by an approved testing laboratory. It is my understanding that the ultimate testing facility is licensed by the State, and body fluid* specimens will be tested to detect the presence of alcohol and/or other drugs in my body. All testing will be in accordance with standards currently disseminated and approved by the National Institute on Drug Abuse.

I acknowledge that I will be under a one year probationary period commending on the date of my return to active employment with MH Construction. I agree and consent to provide specimens of my body fluids* for testing to determine the presence of alcohol and/or drugs whenever requested by the company during my one year probationary period, In addition, during my probationary period and throughout my continued employment with MH Construction, in the event I am directly involved In a work-related accident, or as part of on-site drug screening, or it there is reason to suspect my use of drugs or alcohol intoxications, I agree and consent to provide specimens of my body fluids* for testing to determine the presence or alcohol and/or drugs.

It is agreed that, upon request, I will be furnished results of tests performed on my body fluids* specimen by the Company or the testing laboratory. The testing laboratory is only authorized to notify MH Construction whether a test is POSITIVE or NEGATIVE in accordance with predetermined threshold levels.

*Body fluids tests will normally utilize only urine specimens. Test which entails the withdrawal of blood will be exercised only in situations involving an injury accident where I am rendered unconscious and unable to provide a urine specimen, and I agree and consent to such a test under those circumstances.

I acknowledge that I have read and understand MH Construction’s Drug and Alcohol Policy. I agree to cooperate fully with any and all Company drug and alcohol testing requests, and I understand that my failure to cooperate fully, or submit to a drug or alcohol test when requested by the Company will result in my immediate termination of employment with MH Construction.
