_______________________

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Mh Construction Industrial Safety And Health Program

Title

Fire Prevention

Number

Mish 13

Revision

1

Approved By

Jeremy Thamert 03/01/2025

Name Date

Effective Date

3/1/2025

Page

1 of 4

1.0 PURPOSE

To establish the Contractor’s minimum requirements for fire protection/prevention programs. Programs shall comply with Occupational Safety and Health Administration/Washington Industrial Safety and health Act (OSHA/WISHA), and the National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) requirements.

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 Key Supervisor shall notify MH Construction Safety and the Owner when a fire has been reported or a fire extinguisher has been used.

2.2 The Key Supervisor is responsible for the following:

2.2.1 Promptly identifying and reporting to MH Construction all fire protection impairments such as:

 Planned tests, inspections, modification, and/or maintenance operations that will result in impairment of fire protection systems. This notification shall be completed prior to starting the activity.

 Unplanned or emergency fire protection system or equipment impairments.

 Notifying MH Construction when a fire extinguisher is used for any reason.

 Immediately reporting fires by activating a fire alarm pull box or calling the Fire Department by regular telephone (dial 911).

2.2.2 Orienting employees to emergency procedures and locations of emergency equipment (i.e., fire extinguishers, fire alarm pull boxes, and fire exits).

2.2.3 Supervising their work areas to ensure compliance with fire protection program requirements.

2.3 Employees are responsible for the following:

2.3.1 Maintaining good housekeeping at work areas and keeping work areas free of fire hazards.

2.3.2 Informing supervision of unsafe fire/safety conditions.

2.3.3 Immediately reporting fires by activating a fire alarm pull box or calling the Fire Department by phone (dial 911).

2.3.4 Notifying their supervisors whenever a fire extinguisher is used.

2.3.5 Being aware of, and understanding the fire prevention/protection measures, pre-fire plans, and safety rules in their work areas.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Competent Person: One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt, corrective measures to eliminate them. A Competent Person is also one who has extensive knowledge/experience in a particular activity or job function.

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 Methods, practices and inspections relating to fire prevention shall comply with the following statutory requirements:

 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 1926, Subpart F; Fire Protection and prevention

 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 1910, Subpart L; Fire Protection.

 Washington Administrative Code (WAC) 296-155, Part D; Fire Protection and Prevention.

4.2 Training shall be provided to employees on fire prevention, response to fires including emergency procedures and locations of emergency equipment, (i.e., fire extinguishers, fire alarm pull boxes, and fire exits), and general knowledge of extinguishers.

4.3 Fire protection equipment (fire extinguishers) shall be inspected monthly and have a qualitative inspection annually. A tag recording the inspections shall be attached to each extinguisher and tested.

4.4 Fire protection impairments shall be immediately identified and reported to MH Construction Key Supervisor in areas where the contractor is working.

4.5 Emergency egress shall be maintained with no obstructions.

4.6 All fires shall be immediately reported to MH Construction Safety.

5.0 PROCEDURE

5.1 FIRE EXTINGUISHERS: Portable fire extinguishers shall be located throughout facilities according to OSHA/WISHA requirements. These are first response devices and designed for use on small fires.

5.1.1 A competent person shall be designated in writing. See MISH 3.

5.1.2 The competent person shall conduct documented inspections of all firefighting equipment, emergency lighting, self-illuminating exit signs, and fire extinguishers monthly. Inspection tags shall be completed and attached as documentation of the inspections. Defective equipment shall be replaced immediately.

5.1.3 Initial and annual portable fire extinguisher training shall be required for employees. The training shall include knowledge of fire hazards, the correct type of extinguisher to use, and the general principles of fire extinguishers. The training shall include “hands on training”. The training shall include actual discharge of a fire extinguisher.

5.2 HOUSEKEEPING: The work areas shall be kept free from the accumulation of unnecessary combustible, flammable material.

5.2.1 All storage areas shall be kept neat and orderly.

5.2.2 Driveways, 15 feet wide, shall be provided between and around combustible storage piles. Driveways shall be maintained free of rubbish and equipment accumulation.

5.2.3 Combustible scrap, garbage, and other wastes accumulated during construction activities shall be removed at regular intervals.

5.2.4 Contractors shall provide containers for the collection and separation of waste, trash, oily and used rags, and other refuse.

NOTE: Covers shall be provided for containers used for garbage, oily, and flammable wastes.

5.3 SPECIAL FIRE PROTECTION REQUIREMENTS

5.3.1 Fire Lanes: Adequate fire lanes shall be maintained to permit Fire Department access to buildings or equipment.

5.3.2 Welding, Cutting, and Heating Permits: Welding/burning permits are required and shall be prepared for welding, cutting, and heating operations (refer to MISH 14).

5.3.3 Greenhouse Fire Protection: The following requirements shall be met in addition to normal fire prevention procedures in order to provide adequate fire protection to areas enclosed by greenhouses:

5.3.3.1.1 The supporting structure shall be constructed of wood or metal materials.

5.3.3.2 The covering for walls, ceiling, and floors shall be of noncombustible or fire-retardant material. All plastic shall be fire retardant.

5.3.3.3 Combustible trash shall be either placed in metal containers equipped with metal covers, or removed immediately.

5.3.3.4 Approved containers shall be provided for flammable or combustible liquid-soaked cloths, rags, or waste.

5.3.3.5 Trash and rag containers shall be emptied daily.

5.3.3.6 No Smoking shall be allowed inside and within 10 feet of greenhouses.

5.3.3.7 No storage of combustibles within 10 feet of greenhouses shall be allowed.

5.3.4 Flammable Vapors: Planning work for painting, solvent cleaning of parts, etc., shall include provisions (through engineering and/or administrative controls) to ensure that the work area does not accumulate flammable vapors exceeding 10 percent of the lower explosion limit (LEL).

6.0 RECORDS

Document

Record Submittal

Responsibility

Record Retention

Responsibility

Competent Person Designation

Contractor

Construction Management

7.0 REFERENCES

NFPA 30 Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code

NFPA 70 National Electrical Code

8.0 ATTACHMENTS

Not Applicable

MISH 32 — Compressed Gas & Air

Chapter 4: High-Risk Operations | 29 CFR 1926.350 | AGC: Hazard Control

3.0

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Mh Construction Industrial Safety And Health Program

Title

Compressed Gas/Compressed Air

Number

Mish 34

Revision

1

Approved By

Jeremy Thamert 03/01/2025

Name Date

Effective Date

3/1/2025

Page

1 of 6

1.0 PURPOSE

To establish requirement for compressed gas and compressed air, and to ensure compliance with Occupational Safety and Health Administration/Washington Industrial Safety and Health Act (OSHA/WISHA) requirements.

This standard applies to all operations in which compressed gas cylinders are used, stored, transported, or handled, except the following:

  • Fire extinguishers
  • Self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) cylinders
  • Chlorine cylinders
  • Liquified petroleum gas (LPG) cylinders

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 The Key Supervisor shall be responsible for implementing and ensuring compliance with this procedure, including performing the required inspections.

2.2 Employees shall be responsible for complying with the provisions of this procedure.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Not Applicable

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 Compressed gas and compressed air shall be installed, transported, and maintained in accordance with the following statutory requirements:

  • 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 1910, Subpart H; Hazardous Materials
  • 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 1926, Subpart J; Welding and Cutting
  • Washington Administrative Code (WAC) 296-24, Part K; Compressed Gas and Gas Equipment.
  • Washington Administrative Code (WAC) 296-155, Part H; Welding and Cutting

4.2 COMPRESSED GAS CYLINDERS

4.2.1 Cylinders shall not be used for any purpose other than that for which they are designed.

4.2.2 Safety devices in regulators or on cylinder valves shall not be tampered with.

4.2.3 Every cylinder shall be considered to be full and handled accordingly, even if it is empty.

4.2.4 Mixing gases, transferring from one cylinder to another, or refilling gas cylinders shall be prohibited.

4.2.5 Approved reducers and regulators shall be used; gas shall not be used from cylinders without reducing pressure through specifically approved regulators.

4.2.6 Connecting devices shall be free of oil, Teflon tape, and grease, and shall be the proper size for the cylinder (have threads corresponding to those on the cylinder valve).

4.2.7 Cylinders shall not be dragged, dropped, or struck.

4.2.8 Compressed gas cylinders shall be stored in areas that are safe, dry, well ventilated, and protected from the weather. Compressed gas cylinders shall be protected from direct sun and weather conditions. Cylinders shall not be exposed to temperatures above 125º F.

4.2.9 Oxyacetylene gas systems shall be equipped with reverse flow gas check valves on all hoses.

4.2.10 Gaseous hydrogen systems shall be equipped with pressure relief devices and shall be protected from tampering. These relief devices shall be arranged so that if actuated, they have an unobstructed upward vent path to open air.

4.2.11 Cylinders with dents, burns, or other signs of damage shall not be used.

4.2.12 All compressed gas cylinders shall be clearly labeled or tagged to indicate:

  • Date the cylinder was delivered (tag).
  • Date the last hydrostatic test was performed (stamp).
  • Cylinder contents (label with either chemical or trade name).
  • Whether the cylinder is empty (tag empty cylinders as “Empty or “MT”).

4.2.13 Cylinder tags or markings shall not be removed or altered

NOTE: Cylinder markings shall be made using stencils, stamps, or durable labels and tags that are legible and not readily removable. When practical, these markings shall be on the cylinder shoulder. By itself, color-coding is not adequate marking.

4.3 MANIFOLD COMPONENT SELECTION AND INSTALLATION: The entire manifold assembly shall be Factory Mutual (FM) approved or Underwriters Laboratory (UL) listed for use with compressed gases. All manifolds and manifold parts shall be used only for the gas or gases for which they are approved.

4.3.1 Cylinder manifolds shall be installed under the supervision of a person-in-charge who is familiar with the uses of the gas at that facility.

4.3.2 Compressed gas manifolds shall be designed to meet the applicable National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) standard(s).

4.3.3 Flammable gas piping systems shall be grounded and bonded in accordance with the applicable NFPA standard.

4.4 PROCUREMENT SPECIFICATIONS AND LIMITS: Requisitions for cylinders and hardware shall include the following specification when applicable:

4.4.1 Portable cylinders used to store and/or ship compressed gas shall be constructed and maintained in accordance with DOT regulations (U.S. Department of Transportation, 49 CFR, parts 171-179).

4.4.2 Devices used on compressed gas cylinders shall comply with American national Standards Institute and Compressed Gas Association Standards C-4 and V-1.

5.0 PROCEDURE

5.1 GENERAL GAS CYLINDER OPERATING PRECAUTIONS

5.1.1 Cylinder valves shall be opened slowly.

5.1.2 To ensure that the valve can be shut quickly a key, handle, or nonadjustable wrench shall be installed on cylinder valve stems (if the cylinder valve does not have a permanent handwheel).

5.1.3 When the work is complete, all valves and regulators shall be closed and the gas lines and regulators are drained. The adjusting screw shall be “backed out”. (This does not include manifold gases.)

5.1.4 Cylinders shall be secured upright and protected against being knocked over, or are secured in the position suggested by the manufacturer.

5.1.5 Difficult connections shall not be forced. The threads on a regulator or union shall match those on the cylinder valve outlet.

5.1.6 Only regulators and pressure gauges on the gases for which they are designed and intended shall be used. Repair, modification, or alteration on cylinders, valves, or attachments shall be performed by the manufacturer/authorized service center.

5.1.7 Before connecting to a cylinder valve outlet, the cylinder valve shall be “cracked” for an instant to clear any dust or dirt from the opening and then the valve is shut. (Always direct the valve nozzle away from yourself and others before “cracking” the valve.)

5.1.8 Acetylene cylinder valves shall be preferably opened three-fourths (3/4) of a turn or less; acetylene cylinder valves shall not be opened more than one and one-half (1-1/2) turns.

5.1.9 Compressed gas cylinders shall be secured upright in an approved manner, with each cylinder secured snugly.

5.2 STORAGE REQUIREMENTS

5.2.1 Cylinders containing oxidizing gases shall be stored away from flammable substances (such as oil and volatile liquids).

5.2.2 Cylinder shall be secured and stored upright and, for cylinders that can be capped, with the protective valve cap installed hand-tight.

5.2.3 Cylinders shall not be stored near elevators, gangways, stairwells, or any other places where they are likely to be knocked down or interfere with traffic.

5.2.4 Limits shall be observed on the number of cylinders allowed in storage (inside or outside) set by the applicable NFPA standard.

5.2.5 Smoking shall be prohibited within 25 ft. of flammable gas cylinder storage areas. Storage areas containing flammable gases shall be posted “NO SMOKING.”

5.2.6 Empty cylinder shall be segregated from other cylinders and labeled or tagged “Empty” or “MT.” The cylinder valve on each empty cylinder shall be shut, and the cylinder capped (where possible).

5.2.7 Acetylene cylinders shall be stored valve end up.

.

5.2.8 Oxygen cylinders not in use shall not be stored within 20 ft. of cylinders containing flammable gases or combustible materials unless the two kinds of cylinders are separated by a fire wall that is at least five feet tall and has a fire-resistance rating of at least one hour.

5.3 HANDLING/TRANSPORT

5.3.1 Cylinders shall be secured on an approved cylinder cart, their regulators removed, and the valve-protection caps installed before moving the cylinders.

5.3.2 Cylinders without valve protection caps (e.g., inert gas cylinders such as 10 and 40 cubic foot capacity cylinders, #1 and #2 oxyacetylene bottles, etc.) shall have their regulators protected by securing the cylinder upright.

5.3.3 All cylinder valves shall be checked to ensure they are shut before moving the cylinders.

5.3.4 Compressed gas cylinders shall be secured to a cradle, boat, or suitable platform before they are lifted by crane or derrick. Cylinder shall not be lifted by magnetic devices or by attaching hooks or straps to the protective caps: instead, they are hoisted with an approved lifting device (such as a cage-type lifting platform with a rail for securing the cylinders).

5.3.5 Cylinders shall be rolled for only short (less than 15 ft.) distances using the curve of the bottom cylinder edge, but shall not be dragged across the ground. The valve protection cap shall be installed before cylinder is rolled.

5.3.6 A cylinder hand cart shall be used to transport cylinders more than 15 ft. Cylinders shall be secured upright to the cart firmly to ensure that the cylinders are not dropped. The valve protection cap shall be installed before a cylinder is moved.

5.3.7 When moving cylinders, they shall be protected from cuts or abrasions.

6.0 RECORDS

Not Applicable

7.0 REFERENCES

Compressed Gas Association Standards (ass applicable)

49 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR), Parts 171-179; Department of Transportation

Requirements for hazardous Materials

8.0 ATTACHMENTS

Not Applicable

OSHA Reference: https://www.osha.gov/construction

OSHA Reference: https://www.osha.gov/laws-regs/regulations/standardnumber/1926

Mh Construction Industrial Safety And Health Program

Title

Motor Vehicle Safety Program

Number

Mish 18

Revision

1

Approved By

Jeremy Thamert 03/01/2025

Name Date

Effective Date

3/1/2025

Page

1 of 4

1.0 PURPOSE

To establish the requirements and responsibilities for employees operating motor vehicles. Further, to ensure compliance with Occupational Safety and Health Administration/Washington Industrial Safety and health Act (OSHA/WISHA) requirements.

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 The Division Manager shall be responsible for the following:

2.1.1 Ensuring drivers have a current state driver’s license with the proper endorsements.

2.1.2 Investigating motor vehicle accidents in accordance with MISH 8.

2.1.3 Ensuring permits are obtained for oversized loads.

2.1.4 Reporting vehicle accidents to MH Construction Safety.

2.2 Employees shall be responsible for complying with the provisions of this procedure.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Authorized Drivers: An employee who has authorized the release of their driving record and after review has been cleared by the division manager to drive a company vehicle.

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 Each employee driving a vehicle shall have in his/her possession a valid state driver’s license (with the proper endorsement) for the type of vehicle driven.

4.2 Professional drivers shall have a current Certified Driver’s License (CDL), with the proper endorsements. The Division Manager or lower-tier shall ensure that professional drivers with CDLs comply with applicable State and Federal requirements to keep CDLs in force.

4.3 Motor vehicles shall have seat (safety) belts in accordance with the Washington State Law.

4.4 Drivers and passengers riding in vehicles which are equipped with seat belts shall wear their seat belts at all times while the vehicle is in motion.

4.5 Motor vehicle accidents shall be investigated and reported as required in MISH 8.

4.6 Loads wider than eight feet six inches, higher than 14 feet or longer than 40 feet shall have an oversized load permit

4.7 All accidents involving motor vehicles shall be reported to the MH Construction Safety Department.

4.8 Only authorized drivers shall be allowed to drive a company owned vehicle. Check with main office to determine if an employee has been cleared to drive.

4.9 No car-pooling to and from work in company vehicles. Division Manager may make exception on a case by case. If passenger is not an employee and it is not work related the authorized vehicle operator shall have supplemental insurance to cover all non employees riding in the vehicle.

4.10 All employees must have permission to haul any type of company goods, including company owned tools in their personal vehicles to and from jobsites.

4.11 Operating vehicle under the influence of drugs or alcohol is strictly prohibited.

4.12 Company vehicle use on out-of-town jobs will be specific to that job. Project Managers/Superintendents are responsible for providing special guidelines that may apply.

4.13 The Vehicle operator is responsible to ensure proper insurance cards and tabs are on or in the vehicle.

4.14 Any violations associated with the company owned vehicle shall be reported to supervisor and safety immediately. The employee is responsible for all cost associated with the violation.

4.15 The use of company vehicles out side of work related activities is prohibited, unless approved by the operator’s division manager.

5.0 PROCEDURE

5.1 OPERATOR CARE

5.1.1 Vehicle drivers shall inspect and test the essential controls and safety equipment before placing the vehicle in motion. Drivers shall report any unsatisfactory condition to their supervisor. If the driver believes a vehicle is unsafe to drive, it shall not be driven.

5.1.2 Vehicles shall be operated in accordance with the motor vehicle laws of the State of Washington.

5.1.3 Vehicle drivers shall not wear any devices, which restrict or impair hearing or vision (e.g., radio headphones).

5.1.4 No vehicle shall be loaded in a manner, which obscures the driver’s view in any direction or interferes with the safe operation of the vehicle.

5.1.5 Vehicle loads shall be secure, and vehicles shall not be loaded beyond their rated capacity.

5.1.6 No vehicle shall be left unattended until the engine is shut off, the parking brake securely set, and the gear selector placed in the “park” position (on automatic transmissions) or the lowest gear position (on manual transmission).

5.1.7 It shall be the driver’s responsibility to ensure safe passage of the vehicle and load under power lines or other overhead obstructions.

5.1.8 Vehicle drivers shall not permit any person to ride on equipment that is not specifically designed for carrying passengers.

5.1.9 During periods of poor weather or deteriorated road conditions, vehicle

shall reduce speed, increase following distances, and use other defensive driving measures appropriate to the situation.

5.1.10 No vehicle shall be put in motion until all window surfaces are clear of obstructions (e.g., snow, ice, frost).

5.1.11 When backing, a reliable guide shall be used in congested areas or where vision is obstructed. Backing should always be done slowly, looking for other vehicles in the area.

5.1.12 Trucks which are parked on an incline shall be chocked.

5.2 OFF-ROAD VEHICLE SAFETY

5.2.1 Vehicles are only driven off-road when required by job assignment. Adverse impact to the environment shall be kept to a minimum.

5.2.2 Off-road vehicles have mufflers and are equipped with spark arrestor.

5.2.3 Vehicles equipped with catalytic converters are not parked over dry grass, brush, etc.

5.2.4 Off-road vehicles are equipped with a radio transmitter/receiver (or cellular telephone), fire extinguisher and shovel.

5.2.5 Off-road vehicles are equipped with headlights and taillights for night use.

5.2.6 Off-road driving is curtailed to mission-essential activities during the grass fire season, normally May through September.

6.0 RECORDS

Not Applicable

7.0 REFERENCES

MISH 8 Event Reporting: Incidents, Accidents, and Near Misses

WAC 296-155 Washington Industrial Safety and Health Act Safety Standards for Construction

8.0 ATTACHMENTS

Not Applicable

MISH 34 — Distracted Driving & Mobile Device Policy

Chapter 5: Equipment & Vehicle Safety | 29 CFR 1926.600 | Travelers Audit Rec 26-3

DOCUMENT

REVISION

EFFECTIVE DATE

APPROVED BY

MISH 34

3.0

April 2026

Jeremy Thamert

President

Safety Officer

Travelers Risk Control Consultant

AGC Representative

Jeremy Thamert

Matt Ramsey

_______________________

_______________________

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

TRAVELERS AUDIT FINDING 26-3 — ACTION REQUIRED

A formal policy to reduce distraction by mobile technology devices while driving was

not in place as of the April 8, 2026 Travelers Risk Control consultation.

This section fulfills that requirement. All employees who drive on behalf of MHC

must read, sign, and comply with this policy.

1.0 PURPOSE & SCOPE

1.1 This policy establishes MH Construction's requirements for the safe use of mobile technology devices while operating any vehicle on behalf of the company.

1.2 This policy applies to all employees, subcontractors, and agents who operate company-owned, rented, leased, borrowed, or personally-owned vehicles for MHC business.

1.3 Mobile technology devices include: cellular phones, smartphones, tablets, laptops, GPS/navigation systems, portable audio/video players, and any other electronic device.

2.0 POLICY REQUIREMENTS

2.1 Prohibited Actions While Driving

  • Handheld use of any cellular phone or mobile device while the vehicle is in motion.
  • Sending, reading, or composing text messages or emails while driving.
  • Dialing a phone number while driving.
  • Viewing video, television, or DVDs while driving.
  • Inputting data into any device (GPS, laptop, tablet) while driving.
  • Taking photos or video while driving.

2.2 Hands-Free Use

2.2.1 Hands-free cellular calls are discouraged while driving. If a call must be taken, it should be brief. Extended conversations must be deferred until the vehicle is parked in a safe location.

2.2.2 Employees are not required to answer calls while driving. Voicemail is the preferred method for non-emergency communications while in transit.

2.2.3 Supervisors and managers must not contact employees by phone or text when they know the employee is driving, unless it is a genuine emergency.

CRITICAL FIELD REMINDER — DISTRACTED DRIVING STATISTICS

Nearly 80% of all crashes involved driver inattention within 3 seconds of the incident.

At highway speeds, 5 seconds of inattention = driving a full football field without looking.

Source: Virginia Tech Transportation Institute / NHTSA

3.0 COMPLIANCE & ENFORCEMENT

3.1 All employees who drive on MHC business must sign the Mobile Device Policy Acknowledgment Form (Form 34-A) before operating any vehicle for MHC.

3.2 Violation of this policy is subject to disciplinary action up to and including termination, consistent with MHC's progressive discipline policy.

3.3 Employees who receive a citation for mobile device use while driving on MHC business must report it to the Safety Officer within 24 hours.

3.4 Supervisors who observe violations must document and report them to the Safety Officer. Failure to report a known violation is itself a policy violation.

4.0 TRAINING

4.1 All employees who drive on MHC business must complete distracted driving awareness training prior to operating vehicles for MHC.

4.2 Training must be documented in the employee's personnel file and renewed annually.

4.3 Travelers Risk Control provides free distracted driving training resources for MHC employees.

5.0 STATE LAW COMPLIANCE

5.1 All employees must comply with Washington State law RCW 46.61.672, which prohibits handheld cell phone use while driving.

5.2 Washington State law also prohibits reading, writing, or sending electronic messages while driving (RCW 46.61.668).

5.3 Fines and penalties resulting from mobile device violations while driving on MHC business are the responsibility of the employee.

WISHA: RCW 46.61.672 / RCW 46.61.668 is more stringent than federal OSHA for handheld device prohibition while driving. WAC 296-155

FORM 34-A — MOBILE DEVICE POLICY ACKNOWLEDGMENT

I have read, understand, and agree to comply with MH Construction's Distracted Driving & Mobile Device Policy (MISH 34). I understand that violation of this policy may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination.

I understand that I am not required to answer my phone while driving, and that I should pull over to a safe location before making or receiving calls.

TRAVELERS AUDIT FINDING 26-1 — ACTION REQUIRED

Motor vehicle records (MVRs) were not being reviewed for all drivers as of the

April 8, 2026 Travelers Risk Control consultation.

This section establishes the MHC MVR review program. Compliance is required

for all employees who operate vehicles on behalf of MH Construction.

1.0 PURPOSE & SCOPE

1.1 MH Construction requires Motor Vehicle Record (MVR) checks for all employees who operate vehicles on behalf of the company, including company-owned, rented, leased, borrowed, or personally-owned vehicles.

1.2 MVR reviews help identify drivers with poor driving histories before incidents occur, reducing liability and protecting employees and the public.

1.3 This program applies to all MHC employees, including supervisors, project managers, and field personnel who drive on MHC business.

2.0 MVR REVIEW REQUIREMENTS

2.1 Pre-Employment: An MVR must be obtained and reviewed for all candidates who will drive on MHC business before they begin employment.

2.2 Annual Review: MVRs must be reviewed annually for all active drivers. Reviews must be documented and retained in the employee's personnel file.

2.3 Post-Incident: An MVR must be obtained following any vehicle accident involving an MHC employee, regardless of fault.

2.4 MVRs must provide at least a three-year driving history and include all violations and accidents, not just those occurring on the job.

3.0 DISQUALIFYING CRITERIA

The following driving record characteristics within the past three years constitute a POOR DRIVING RECORD. Employees with poor driving records shall not be permitted to operate vehicles on MHC's behalf:

Disqualifying Criterion

Category

Conviction for alcohol and/or drug-related driving offense (DUI/DWI)

Criminal

Serious moving violation (excessive speed, reckless/careless driving)

Moving Violation

3 or more minor moving violations, at-fault accidents, or preventable accidents

Pattern

License suspension, revocation, or administrative restriction

License Status

Leaving the scene of an accident as defined by state law

Criminal

At-fault in a fatal accident

Severity

Felony committed involving a vehicle

Criminal

4.0 DOCUMENTATION & RETENTION

4.1 All MVRs must be retained in the employee's personnel file for a minimum of three years.

4.2 The Safety Officer is responsible for maintaining the MVR review schedule and ensuring all reviews are completed on time.

4.3 MVR review results and any resulting actions (reassignment, termination) must be documented.

4.4 These criteria must be communicated in writing to all employees who drive on MHC business.

5.0 EMPLOYEE CONSENT

5.1 All employees who drive on MHC business must sign Form 35-A (MVR Authorization & Consent) authorizing MHC to obtain their driving record.

FORM 35-A — MVR AUTHORIZATION & CONSENT

I hereby authorize MH Construction, Inc. to obtain my Motor Vehicle Record (MVR) from the appropriate state agency. I understand this review will be conducted prior to my employment and annually thereafter, and following any vehicle accident in which I am involved while on MHC business.

I understand that a poor driving record as defined in MISH 35 may result in my reassignment or termination from driving duties.

Employee Signature: _____________________________________________

Driver's License Number: _____________________________________________

State of Issue: _____________________________________________

Date: _____________________________________________

Mh Construction Industrial Safety And Health Program

Title

Equipment Maintenance And Inspection

Number

Mish 19

Revision

1

Approved By

Jeremy Thamert 03/01/2025

Name Date

Effective Date

3/1/2025

Page

1 of 9

1.0 PURPOSE

To establish the system to inspect and maintain vehicles and heavy construction equipment.

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 The Key Supervisor shall be responsible for the following:

2.1.1 Maintaining vehicles and equipment in accordance with applicable Occupational Safety and Health Administration/Washington Industrial Safety and health Act (OSHA/WISHA) and American national Standards Institute (ANSI) standards.

2.1.2 Conducting inspections of equipment, and correcting deficiencies noted during the inspections.

2.2 Employees shall be responsible for conducting daily inspections of equipment they are operating, and for reporting deficiencies to their supervisor.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Heavy Construction Equipment: Mobile cranes, crawler cranes, tractors, compactors, fork lifts, graders, scrapers, off-highway trucks, etc.

Motor Vehicles: Any vehicles that operate on a construction site.

Preventive Maintenance: Practice of utilizing periodic inspections, adjustments, lubrication, and minor repair of equipment for the purposes of extending its useful life and reducing the frequency and magnitude of repairs.

Scheduled Maintenance: Regularly scheduled inspections, tests, lubrications, adjustments, cleaning, and tightening of various components of a piece of equipment.

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 Equipment maintenance and inspection shall comply with the following statutory requirements:

  • 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 1926, Subpart O; Motor Vehicles, Mechanized Equipment and Marine Operations
  • 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 1910, Subpart N; Materials Handling and Storage
  • 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 1910, Subpart F; Powered Platforms, Manlifts, and Vehicle-Mounted Work Platforms
  • Washington Administrative Code (WAS) 296-155, Part M; Motor Vehicles, Mechanized Equipment and Marine Operations.

4.2 On-site preventive maintenance of equipment and vehicles shall be performed by qualified personnel.

4.3 Motor vehicles and heavy equipment shall be maintained and inspected monthly in conformance to the manufacturer’s recommendations, and documented on equipment-specific safety check and/or inspection report forms (see attachments 19-A).

4.4 Prior to heavy equipment being brought onto a project, an inspection of the equipment shall be performed and complete the applicable safety check and inspection report forms. The forms shall be submitted to maintenance department. Deficiencies noted during the inspection shall be corrected prior to the equipment being brought onto the project.

4.5 MH Construction shall notify the owner or general contractor when heavy equipment is to be brought onto a project.

4.6 The key supervisor shall maintain manufacturer’s recommendations and inspection checklists onsite for review.

5.0 PROCEDURE

5.1 MOTOR VEHICLES

5.1.1 Vehicles being operated shall be checked at the beginning of each shift to ensure that the following parts, equipment, and accessories are in safe operating condition and free of apparent damage that could cause failure while in use.

  • Brakes (including trailer brake connections, parking brake, and emergency stopping systems.
  • Tires
  • Horn
  • Steering mechanism
  • Coupling devices
  • Seat belts
  • Operating controls
  • Safety devices

5.1.2 Lights, reflectors, windshield wipers, defrosters, fire extinguishers, steps, and hand-holds for vehicle access shall also be inspected where such equipment is necessary.

5.1.3 Defects noted during the motor vehicle inspection shall be corrected before the vehicle is placed into service.

5.2 HEAVY CONSTRUCTION EQUIPMENT: The following are specific requirements relating to heavy equipment.

5.2.1 Crawler, locomotive, and truck cranes in use shall meet the applicable requirements for design, inspection, construction and testing, maintenance and operation as prescribed in the ANSI B30.5.

NOTE: Daily inspections shall be conducted on all critical items such as brakes, crane hooks, ropes, hydraulic and pneumatic cylinders, and hydraulic and pneumatic relief pressure valves. These inspections shall be documented and the records kept available for review.

5.2.2 Lift Trucks: Operation of powered industrial trucks may be hazardous if maintenance is neglected or repairs, rebuilds, or adjustments are not performed in accordance with the manufacturer’s design criteria.

5.2.2.1 A scheduled, planned maintenance, lubrication, and inspection system shall be followed on all powered industrial trucks.

5.2.2.2 In an industrial truck is found to be in need or repair or is in any way unsafe or contributes to an unsafe condition, the truck shall not be operated until it has been restored to safe operating condition.

5.2.2.3 Only qualified operators are allowed to operate lift trucks.

MISH 37 — Aerial Lifts & Elevated Work Platforms

Chapter 5: Equipment & Vehicle Safety | 29 CFR 1926.453 | AGC: Fall Protection

DOCUMENT

REVISION

EFFECTIVE DATE

APPROVED BY

MISH 37

3.0

April 2026

Jeremy Thamert

President

Safety Officer

Travelers Risk Control Consultant

AGC Representative

Jeremy Thamert

Matt Ramsey

_______________________

_______________________

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Mh Construction Industrial Safety And Health Program

Title

Aerial Lifts And Elevating Work Platforms

Number

Mish 20

Revision

1

Approved By

Jeremy Thamert 03/01/2025

Name Date

Effective Date

3/1/2025

Page

1 of 4

1.0 PURPOSE

To define the system for safe operation and maintenance of elevated work platforms and aerial lifts, and to ensure compliance Occupational Safety and Health Administration/Washington Industrial Safety and Health Act (OSHA/WISHA) requirements.

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 The Key Supervisor shall be responsible for ensuring compliance with this procedure.

2.2 Employees shall be responsible for compliance with the provisions of this procedure.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Aerial Lift: Vehicle-mounted aerial devices used to elevate personnel for work sites aboveground, including the following:

  • Extending and articulating boom platforms
  • Aerial ladders
  • Vertical towers
  • A combination of any of the above

NOTE: These devices are considered aerial lifts regardless of rotating capacity or power source.

Boom-Supported Elevating Work Platform: A type of aerial lift commonly known as the “JLG” manlift.

Self-Propelled Elevating Work Platform: A type of aerial lift commonly known as the “scissor” type aerial lift.

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 Aerial lifts and elevating work platforms shall comply with the following statutory requirements:

  • 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 1926, Subpart N; Cranes, Derricks, Hoists, Elevators and Conveyors
  • 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 1910, Subpart F; Powered Platforms, manlifts, and Vehicle-Mounted Work Platforms
  • Washington Administrative Code (WAC) 296-155, Part J; Ladders, Scaffolds and Elevating Work Platforms

4.2 Personnel who operate elevated work platforms and aerial lifts shall be trained to the requirements of this procedure. Only authorized persons are allowed to operate the equipment.

4.3 Lift controls shall be tested by the user each day prior to use to determine that controls are in safe condition.

4.4 Fall protection for employees in vehicle-mounted articulating or extensible boom type elevating and rotating aerial devices (i.e., JLGs) shall be in accordance with the following:

4.4.1 Workers shall stand on the basket floor, and shall not sit or climb on the edge or handrail. Workers also shall not use planks, ladders, or other devices for a work position.

4.4.2 A full body harness and lanyard shall be worn and attached to the manufactured attachment point.

4.4.3 Tying-off to an adjacent pole, structure, or equipment while working in an aerial lift is prohibited.

4.5 Fall protection for employees on self-propelled elevating work platforms (scissor lifts) shall be in accordance with the following:

4.5.1 Self-propelled elevating work platforms (scissor lifts) shall be provided with standard guardrail or the equivalent.

4.5.2 Personnel shall maintain a firm footing on the platform while working thereon and be secured by a safety harness and lanyard affixed to manufacturer-approved attachment points.

4.6 Boom and basket load limits posted on the unit shall not be exceeded.

4.7 Elevating work platforms or aerial lifts shall not be used to hoist materials that would otherwise require special lifting equipment such as chain-falls, forklifts, cranes, etc. Only material that can be readily handled by the employee using the work platform shall be lifted or moved using the platform. Additionally, only those materials that will be readily utilized (i.e., within two hours) shall be taken on a platform at one time.

4.8 Aerial lift trucks shall not be moved when the boom is elevated in a working position with personnel in the basket. The only exception is equipment specifically designed for this type of operation.

4.9 Articulating boom and extensible boom platforms shall have both upper and lower controls. Lower controls shall be capable of overriding upper controls. All controls shall be plainly marked as to their function.

4.10 The brakes shall be set and outriggers shall be fully extended and positioned on pads or a solid surface. Wheel chokes shall be used when working on inclines.

4.11 Operators shall ensure that the area surrounding the work platform is clear of personnel and equipment before lowering the platform.

4.12 The user shall inspect aerial lifts and elevating work platforms (based on the manufacturer’s manual) prior to use. Defective equipment shall be reported to the supervisor/manager, tagged out of service, and not used until repaired. No modifications to equipment unless written approval from the manufacture.

4.13 Aerial lifts shall be inspected daily. Limit switches shall be checked weekly.

4.14 Applicable safety check forms (see MISH 19) shall be completed during daily inspections. Inspections cover, but are not limited to, the following:

  • Steps and step fastenings
  • Rails, including their supports and fastenings
  • Roller and Slides
  • Belts and belt tension
  • Manholds and fastenings
  • Floor landings
  • Guardrail
  • Lubrication
  • Limit switches
  • Warning signs and lights
  • Illumination
  • Drive pulleys and supports
  • Brakes
  • Electrical switches

4.15 Manufacturer’s maintenance and operators manuals shall be provided with each work platform.

4.16 Equipment will have a working back-up alarm, or a spotter will be used when backing.

4.17 For lines rated 50 kV or below, minimum clearance between the lines and any part of the equipment or load shall be at least 10 feet. If the aerial lift is insulated for the voltage involved, and if the work is performed by a qualified person, the clearance distance (between the uninsulated portion of the aerial lift and the power line) may be referenced to the distance provided in 1910.333(c)(3)(ii)(C) Table S-5.

5.0 PROCEDURE

Not Applicable.

6.0 RECORDS

Responsibility

sections.title: Crane Suspended Work Platforms

sections.body:
MISH 38 — Crane Suspended Work Platforms

Chapter 5: Equipment & Vehicle Safety | 29 CFR 1926.1431 | AGC: Hazard Control

DOCUMENT

REVISION

EFFECTIVE DATE

APPROVED BY

MISH 38

3.0

April 2026

Jeremy Thamert

President

Safety Officer

Travelers Risk Control Consultant

AGC Representative

Jeremy Thamert

Matt Ramsey

_______________________

_______________________

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Mh Construction Industrial Safety And Health Program

Title

Crane Suspended Work Platforms

Number

Mish 21

Revision

1

Approved By

Jeremy Thamert 03/01/2025

Name Date

Effective Date

3/1/2025

Page

1 of 8

1.0 PURPOSE

To define the responsibilities and requirements for protecting personnel engaged in the use of crane-suspended personnel platforms, and to ensure compliance with Occupational Safety and Health Administration/Washington Industrial Safety and Health Act (OSHA/WISHA) requirements.

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 MH Construction Industrial Safety and health (IS&H) shall review and approve all uses of crane suspended personnel platforms.

2.2 The Key Supervisor shall be responsible for the following:

2.2.1 Ensuring employee compliance with this procedure.

2.4 Employees shall be responsible for complying with this procedure.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Qualified Person: One who, by possession of a recognized degree, certificate, or professional standing, or who by extensive knowledge, training, and experience, has successfully demonstrated his/her ability to solve or resolve problems relating to the subject matter, the work, or the project.

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 Crane-suspended work platforms shall comply with the following statutory requirements:

  • 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 1926, Subpart N; Cranes, Derricks, Hoists, Elevators and Conveyors
  • Washington Administrative Code (WAC) 296-155, Part L; Cranes, Derricks, Hoists, Elevators and Conveyors
  • Hanford Hoisting and Rigging Manual

4.2 PRE-JOB REQUIREMENTS

4.2.1 The use of a crane to hoist employees on a personnel platform shall be prohibited, except when conventional means of reaching the work location present a greater hazard or are not possible because of structural design or worksite conditions. The Key Supervisor and MH Construction IS&H shall make a case-by-case evaluation to determine if an alternate method can be used. A justification for the use of crane-suspended personnel platforms shall be written into the Job Hazard Analysis (JHA) and approved by the MH Construction IS&H.

4.2.2 The crane to be used shall have been inspected within the past month. If the crane was out of service, it shall receive a complete annual inspection prior to placement in service.

4.2.3 The crane operator shall conduct a daily documented inspection prior to the start of work each shift.

4.2.4 Cranes with telescoping booms shall be equipped with a device to indicate clearly to the operator at all times the boom-extended length, or an accurate determination of the load radius to be used during the lift shall be made prior to hoisting personnel. A positive acting device shall be used which prevents contact between the load block or overall ball and the boom tip (anti-two-blocking device). This device shall deactivate the hoisting action before damage occurs. Friction type cranes shall be equipped with a two-block warning device.

4.2.5 A meeting attended by the crane operator, signal person, person(s) to be lifted, and the person responsible for the task to be performed shall be held to review work procedures, including the JHA. This meeting shall be held prior to the “trial lift” at each new work location, and shall be repeated for any employees newly assigned to the operation.

4.2.6 Prior to lifting employees, the operator and signal person shall conduct a full-cycle “trial lift” with an unoccupied personnel platform loaded to the anticipated lift weight. The operator shall determine that all systems, controls, and safety devices are functioning properly. The trial lift shall be repeated prior to hoisting employees whenever the crane is moved and set up in a new location, or returned to a previously used location. A visual inspection of the crane, rigging, and the personnel platform shall be conducted by the designated qualified person (and others at his/her direction) immediately after the trial lift to determine whether the

trial lift has exposed any defective component or structure. Any defects found shall be corrected before hoisting personnel.

4.2.7 The “trial lift” process shall be documented on the Pre-lift Checklist (attachment MISH 21-A).

4.2.8 All newly constructed, modified, or repaired personnel platforms shall be load tested to 125 percent of rated capacity. Modified or repaired platforms shall be re-tested prior to use.

4.2.9 Tag lines shall be used unless their use creates an unsafe condition.

4.2.10 All lifts shall be made in accordance with the manufacturer’s lifting recommendations.

4.2.11 The number of employees to be hoisted shall be kept to a minimum, and in no case shall it exceed four persons.

4.2.12 Employees working on the platform shall be considered as weighing at least 250 pounds each.

4.2.13 The platform shall not be used during high winds, electrical storms, snow, or other adverse weather conditions, which could endanger employees on the platform.

4.3 WORK PLATFORM REUIREMENTS

4.3.1 The personnel platform and suspension system shall be designed by a qualified engineer. The suspension system shall be designed to minimize tipping of the platform due to the movement of employees occupying the platform. The platform shall be capable of supporting its own weight and five times the maximum intended load without failure.

4.3.2 The personnel platform shall be conspicuously posted with the following information:

  • Weight of the empty platform
  • Maximum number of personnel
  • Rated load capacity

4.3.3 The platform shall have standard railings as specified by OSHA/WISHA. The sides of the platform shall be enclosed from floor to mid-rail with either solid construction or expanded metal having openings no greater than ½ inch.

4.3.4 If access gates are installed, they shall swing inward and shall be equipped with a restraining device to prevent accidental opening.

4.3.5 The platform shall have overhead protection when employees are potentially exposed to falling objects.

4.3.6 The platform shall be easily identifiable by high visibility color

4.3.7 All welding of the personnel platform and its components shall be performed by a certified welder familiar with the weld grades, type, and material specified in the platform design.

4.4 BOX-TYPE PLATFORMS: In addition to the requirements of section 4.2, the following applies to box-type platforms:

4.4.1 Lifting bridles on box-type work platforms shall consist of four legs of equal length. One end shall be securely attached to each corner of the platform and the other end securely attached to a common ring, shackle, or other equivalent device to accommodate the crane hook or a strap to the crane hook.

4.4.2 The work platform shall be provided with an inside grabrail.

4.4.3 Each occupant shall wear a body harness in accordance with WISHA, and the lanyard shall be secured to a structural member capable of supporting a fall impact for employees using the anchorage point.

4.4.4 A substantial safety line shall pass through the eye of each leg of the bridle and shall be securely fastened with a minimum amount of slack to the lift line above the headache ball, or to the crane hook itself if a load block is in use (see attachments MISH 21-B and MISH 21-C).

4.4.5 The platform shall be constructed with a minimum five-to-one safety factor. Five hundred pounds shall be allowed for the first person with light tools, with at least an additional 250 pounds for each person added.

4.5 OPERATING REQUIREMENTS

4.5.1 The lift shall be performed under controlled conditions, and under the direction of an appointed signal person.

4.5.2 Communication between crane operator, signal person, and person(s) being lifted shall be maintained through standard hand signals or voice communication equipment. If radio equipment is used, it shall be equipped with a discrete channel.

4.5.3 The crane operator shall remain at the controls at all times when personnel are on the platform.

4.5.4 Lifting or lowering shall not exceed 100 feet (30.48 m) per minute. Downward motion shall always be with power or controlled lowering (no free fall).

4.5.5 Cranes shall not travel with personnel on the platform

4.5.6 The employee(s) being lifted, moved, or positioned shall remain in continuous sight or in contact with the operator or signal person.

4.5.7 Crane outriggers shall be fully extended and locked. A firm footing uniformly level within one percent shall be provided.

4.5.8 The total weight of the lifted load (including personnel) shall not exceed 50 percent of the rated capacity for the radius and configuration of the crane.

4.5.9 The crane hook shall be provided with a safety latch or other self-closing device.

5.0 PROCEDURE

Not Applicable.

6.0 RECORDS

Document

Record Submittal

Responsibility

Record Retention

Responsibility

All Documents

(generated by this procedure, e.g.,

design, welding, maintenance)

Contractor

MH Construction

Pre-lift Checklist

Contractor

MH Construction

7.0 REFERENCES

ANSI B30.2 Cableways, Cranes, Derricks, Hoists, Hooks, Jacks, and Slings-Overhead and Gantry Cranes

8.0 ATTACHMENTS

MISH 21-C Multiple-Part Line block Suspension

MISH 39 — Rigging Procedures

Chapter 5: Equipment & Vehicle Safety | 29 CFR 1926.251 | AGC: Hazard Control

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Mh Construction Industrial Safety And Health Program

Title

Rigging

Number

Mish 35

Revision

1

Approved By

Jeremy Thamert 03/01/2025

Name Date

Effective Date

3/1/2025

Page

1 of 4

1.0 PURPOSE

To define the responsibilities and requirements for protecting personnel engaged in the use of rigging equipment, and to ensure compliance with Occupational Safety and Health Administration/Washington Industrial Safety and Health Act (OSHA/WISHA) requirements.

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 The Key Supervisor shall be responsible for the following:

2.1.1 Ensuring employee compliance with this procedure.

2.4 Employees shall be responsible for complying with this procedure.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

3.1 Qualified Person: One who, by possession of a recognized degree, certificate, or professional standing, or who by extensive knowledge, training, and experience, has successfully demonstrated his/her ability to solve or resolve problems relating to the subject matter, the work, or the project.

3.2 Competent Person: A person who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or work conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has the authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

3.2 Lifting Device: Any machine or device used to lift a load, such as a crane, hoist, chain fall, come-along, jack, jacking system, derrick, monorail hoist, gantry crane, or pulley system.

3.3 Rigging: Any material used to attach loads to a lifting device such as, chain, wire rope, synthetic slings, and miscellaneous hardware.

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 All hoisting and rigging activities shall comply with the following statutory requirements:

  • 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 1926, Subparts H, and R
  • Washington Administrative Code (WAC) 296-155, Parts F

4.2 PRE-JOB REQUIREMENTS

4.2.1 All rigging equipment shall be inspected prior to use, and periodically throughout each use, by a competent person.

4.2.2 All rigging found to be unsatisfactory must be removed from the jobsite and disposed of, or tagged or marked out of service and not to be used for load bearing activities.

4.3 RIGGING REQUIREMENTS

4.3.1 Rigging equipment shall be inspected prior to use on each shift and as necessary during its use to ensure that it is safe.

4.3.2 Rigging equipment shall not be loaded in excess of its recommended safe working load.

4.3.3 Rigging equipment, when not in use, shall be removed from the immediate work area so as not to present a hazard to employees.

4.3.4 Special rigging accessories and rigging hardware shall be marked with the rated capacity.

4.3.5 Alloy steel chains

4.3.5.1 Chains used for overhead lifting shall be proof tested alloy steel, and must be grade 80 and above.

4.3.5.2 Chains shall be inspected prior to each use and frequently through its use. Check for visible damage such as cracks or breaks; excessive wear, nicks, or gouges; stretched chain links or components; evidence of heat damage or weld splatter, or excessive pitting or corrosion. Chains must also have the ability to hinge freely.

4.3.5.3 Welded alloy steel chain slings shall have permanently affixed durable identification stating size, grade, rated capacity, and sling manufacture.

4.3.5.4 Hooks, rings, oblong links, pear-shaped links, welded or mechanical coupling links, or other attachments shall have a rated capacity at least equal to that of the chain.

4.3.5.5 The use of job or shop hooks and links, or makeshift fasteners, formed from bolts, rods, etc., or other such attachments, shall be prohibited.

4.3.5.6 The practice of placing bolts, nails, or cold shuts between two links to shorten chains is prohibited.

4.3.5.7 Splicing broken chains by inserting a bolt between two links, or passing one link through another and inserting a bolt or nail to hold it is prohibited.

4.3.6 Wire Rope Slings

4.3.6.1 Wire rope slings shall be inspected prior to use and frequently through its use. Check for visible damage such as severe localized abrasion or scraping, kinking, crushing, bird caging, heat damage, or severe corrosion; 5 broken wires in one strand or 10 total.

4.3.6.2 Wire rope which has been welded or has been subject to welding of any kind shall not be used.

4.3.6.3 Protruding ends of strands in splices on slings and bridles shall be covered or blunted.

4.3.6.4 Wire rope shall not be secured by knots

4.3.7 Synthetic Webbing (nylon, polyester, and polypropylene)

4.3.7.1 Synthetic webbing shall have permanently affixed, durable identification stating Trademark or Manufacturer, rated capacity for the type of hitch, and the type of material.

4.3.7.2 Synthetic Webbing shall be inspected prior to each use and frequently through use. Check for visible damage such as acid or caustic burns; melting or charring of any part of the sling; holes, tears, cuts, or snags; broken or worn stitching in load bearing splices; excessive abrasive wear; knots in any part of the sling; or discoloration and brittle or stiff areas on any part of the sling.

4.3.7.3 Rated capacity of Synthetic Webbing shall never be exceeded.

4.3.8 Rigging Hardware

4.3.8.1 Hardware shall be marked by the manufacture and inspected prior to each use and frequently through its use. Check for visible heat damage, weld spatter, arc strikes, excessive pitting, corrosion, nicks or gouges; bent, twisted, distorted, stretched, elongated, cracked, or broken load-bearing components; reduction at any point around the body or pin, incomplete pin engagement, or excessive thread damage.

4.3.8.2 The manufacture’s recommendations shall be followed in determining the safe working loads of the various sizes and types.

4.3.8.3 Hardware shall not be modified by welding and/or drilling unless written approval by the manufacturer has been received.

4.3.8.4 No open hooks shall be used to hoist a bucket, cage, spreader, or skip, nor in any circumstances where the dislodgment of the hook could cause risk of injury to workers. A safety-hook, or shackle shall be used in such circumstances.

4.3.8.5 When shackles are used, shackle pins shall be secured to prevent accidental withdrawal.

5.0 PROCEDURE

Not Applicable.

6.0 RECORDS

Document

Record Submittal

Responsibility

Record Retention

Responsibility

7.0 REFERENCES

ASME B30.9 Slings

ASME B30.26 Rigging Hardware

Hanford Site Hoisting and Rigging Manual

Rigging Handbook 3rd Edition

8.0 ATTACHMENTS

N/A

MISH 40 — Forklift & Power Industrial Truck Safety

Chapter 5: Equipment & Vehicle Safety | 29 CFR 1926.602 | AGC: Hazard Control

Jeremy Thamert

President

Jeremy Thamert

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Mh Construction Industrial Safety And Health Program

Title

Forklift/Power Industrial Truck Safety Program

Number

Mish 42

Revision

1

Approved By

Jeremy Thamert 03/01/2025

Name Date

Effective Date

3/1/2025

Page

1 of 6

1.0 PURPOSE

To establish procedures for the safe operation of power industrial trucks. Contractors are required by WAC-296-863 to train and monitor employee operations of power industrial trucks. See WAC-296-863 for a complete list of LNI standards for Power Industrial Truck Safety (http://lni.wa.gov/wisha/rules/pits/).

This program additionally supports compliance with the Occupational Safety and Health Administration Powered Industrial Truck Standard, as found in 29 CFR 1910.178. This program applies to all employees, permanent, or temporary, who are required to operate material-handling equipment, including forklifts, reach trucks, order pickers, and powered pallet jacks. A Forklift Operations Risk Assessment is available in the forms section of this website.

2.0 DEFINITIONS

  • Authorized Operator: An employee who has satisfactorily completed both classroom and operation training on material-handling equipment at the company’s facilities.
  • Load Center: The Horizontal distance from the edge of the load ( or the vertical face of the forks or other attachment) to the loads center of gravity.
  • Rated Capacity: The Maximum weight that the powered industrial truck is designed to lift, as determined by the manufacturer.

3.0 RESPONSIBILITIES

3.1 Powered Industrial Truck Operators

Operators are responsible for the following (and as covered under WAC 296-863):

  • Operating all powered industrial trucks in a safe manner consistent with safe rules of operation.
  • Inspecting powered industrial trucks at the beginning of each work shift and completing the appropriate inspection forms.
  • Reporting all equipment malfunctions and/or maintenance needs to their supervisors immediately. Park lift in safe place, remove key, tag or note problem.

4.0 TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

All personnel who operate forklifts, scissor lifts, boom lifts, powered hand trucks, and tractors are required to have the following training.

Training shall consist of a combination of formal instruction (e.g., lecture, discussion, interactive, computer learning, video tape, written material), practical training (demonstrations performed by the trainer and practical exercises performed by the trainee), and evaluation of the operators performance in the workplace. Someone who is authorized, qualified and determined to be competent shall conduct all training.

5.0 TRAINING PROGRAM TOPICS

Trainings shall include providing information on the following topics:

  • Operating instructions, warnings, and precautions for the types of truck the operator will be authorized to operate;
  • Differences between the truck and the automobile;
  • Truck controls and instrumentation: where they are located, what they do, and how they work;
  • Engine or motor operation;
  • Steering and maneuvering;
  • Visibility (including restrictions due to loading);
  • Fork and attachment adaptation, operation, and use limitations;
  • Vehicle capacity and stability;
  • Any vehicle inspection and maintenance that the operator will be required to perform;
  • Refueling and/or charging and recharging of batteries;
  • Operating limitations;
  • Any other operating instructions, warnings, or precautions, listed in the operators Manuel for the types of vehicle that the employee is being trained to operate.
  • Workplace-related topics:
  • Surface conditions where the vehicle will be operated;
  • Composition of loads to be carried and load stability;
  • Load manipulation, stacking, and unstacking;
  • Pedestrian traffic in areas where the vehicle will be operated;
  • Narrow aisles and other restricted places where the vehicle will be operated;
  • Hazardous (classified) locations where the vehicle will be operated;
  • Ramps and other slope surfaces that could affect the vehicles stability;
  • Closed environments ad other areas where insufficient ventilation or poor vehicle maintenance could cause a buildup of carbon monoxide or diesel exhaust; and
  • Other unique or potentially hazardous environmental conditions in the workplace that could affect safe operation.

6.0 REFRESHER TRAINING REQUIREMENTS

Refresher training, including an evaluation of the effectiveness of that training, shall be conducted to ensure that the operator has the knowledge and skills needed to operate the powered industrial truck safely.

Refresher training will be conducted when:

  • The operator has been observed to operate the vehicle in an unsafe manner;
  • The operator has been involved in an accident or near-miss incident;
  • The operator has received an evaluation that reveals that the operator is not operating the truck safely;
  • The operator is assigned to drive a different type of truck; or
  • A condition in the workplace changes in a manner that could affect safe operation of the truck.

An evaluation of each powered industrial truck operators performance shall be conducted at least once every three years. Employee training records shall be maintained for five years.

7.0 PROGRAM ACTIVITIES

7.1 Equipment Inspection and Maintenance

  • Each powered industrial truck will be inspected before each shift.
  • A file will be maintained that list the shift inspections of equipment. This file will be kept at the Administration Offices.
  • If repairs are needed on a powered industrial truck such that it cannot be safely operated, it will; be taken out of service until the repairs have been performed.
  • After repairs have been completed, the powered industrial truck will be given a performance test to ensure that the equipment is safe to operate.
  • Powered industrial trucks will be kept in clean condition, free of dirt, excess oil and grease.

7.2 Changing and Charging Batteries

  • Equipment will be provided to safely flush and neutralize spilled battery acid and electrolyte.
  • Smoking will be prohibited in all battery-charging areas.
  • Eyewash equipment will be maintained in all charging areas.
  • Precautions will be taken to prevent open flames, sparks, and electric arcs in charging areas.
  • Employees who change and service batteries and handle corrosive liquids will wear the proper Personal Protective Equipment (PPE).

8.0 SAFE WORK PRACTICES

8.1 General Safe Work Practices

  • Only authorized, trained personnel shall operate lift trucks.
  • Before start of each shift, a visual inspection must be conducted. Employees shall not operate an unsafe forklift at any time.
  • Fill fuel tanks out of doors while engine is off.
  • Operators shall drive with both hands on the steering wheel. Horseplay is prohibited. Do not drive with west or greasy hands.
  • No person shall ride as a passenger on a forklift or on the load being carried.
  • A forklift will not be used to elevate a platform or pallet with any person on it, except work platforms especially designed for this purpose. Work platforms must have standard guard rails and must be securely fastened to the forks.
  • No person shall stand or walk under elevated forks.
  • Operators should avoid making jerky starts, quick turns, or sudden stops. The operator will not use reverse as a brake.
  • Slow down on wet and slippery surfaces and at cross aisles or locations where vision is obstructed.
  • Operators entering a building or nearing a blind corner shall make their approach at reduced speed. Sound horn and proceed carefully.
  • Operators shall give pedestrians the right of way always.
  • Operators shall not drive toward any person who is in front of a fixed object or wall.
  • Operators shall not overtake and pass another forklift traveling in the same directions, at intersections, blind spots, or hazardous locations.
  • Operators should not put their fingers, arms, or legs between the uprights of the mast and or beyond the contour of the forklift.
  • Forks should always be placed under the load as far as possible. Do not lift a load with one fork.
  • No load should be moved unless it is absolutely safe and secure.
  • Use extra care when handling long lengths of bar stock, pipe, or other materials.
  • Avoid sharp or fast end-swing.
  • Compressed gas cylinders shall be moved only in special pallets designed for this purpose.
  • When unloading trucks or trailers, the brakes on the vehicle will be set (locked) and the wheels chocked.
  • Forklifts must be safely parked when not in used. The controls shall be neutralized, power shut off, brakes set, key removed, and the forks left in a down position flat on the surface, and not obstructing walkways or aisles.
  • A forklift shall not be left on an incline unless it is safely parked, and the wheels blocked.
  • Only Stable and safely arranged loads will be handled.
  • Only loads within the rated capacity of the powered industrial truck will be handled.

•

  • Operators entering a building or mearing a blind corner shall make their approach at reduced speed. Sound horn and proceed carefully.

8.2 Traveling

  • Speed limits will be observed, and under all travel conditions, a powered industrial truck will be operated at speeds that will permit it to be brought to a stop in a safe manner.
  • Three truck lengths (or two seconds) will be maintained between powered industrial trucks in operation.
  • The powered industrial truck will be kept under control at all times.
  • When vision is obscured, the operator will slow down and sound the horn.
  • If the load blocks the operators view, the powered industrial truck will be driven in the direction that provides the best visibility.
  • The powered industrial truck will cross railroad tracks at a diagonal.
  • The powered industrial truck will be parked 8feet or further from the center line of the railroad tracks.
  • The operator will keep a clear view of the path of travel.
  • The loaded powered industrial truck will be driven with the load upgrade when driving on ascending or descending grades greater than 10%.
  • Dock boards and bridge plates will be properly secured before they are driven over.
  • When the forklift is not carrying a load, the operator shall travel with the forks as low as possible (maximum of 3 inches on paved surfaces). When carrying a load, it should be carried as low as possible (consistent with safe operation, 2 to 6 inches above the surface.)
  • The Forks should not be operated while the forklift is traveling.
  • On a downgrade, the load shall be last and the forks raised only enough to clear the surface.
  • On an upgrade, the load shall be first, and the forks raised only enough to clear the surface.

9.0 RECORDS

Not Applicable

10.0 REFERENCES

MISH 8 Event Reporting: Incidents, Accidents, and Near Misses

WAC 296-155 Washington Industrial Safety and Health Act Safety Standards for Construction

11.0 ATTACHMENTS

Not Applicable

Training & Reference Resources

Travelers Risk Control: https://www.travelers.com/risk-control

Travelers Training Library: https://www.travelers.com/risk-control/safety-education-training

OSHA Reference: https://www.osha.gov/construction

OSHA Reference: https://www.osha.gov/laws-regs/regulations/standardnumber/1926

AGC Safety Resources: https://www.agc.org/learn/education-training/safety-health

WISHA / WA L&I: https://lni.wa.gov/safety-health/

NOTE: WISHA citations apply only where WAC 296-155 is MORE STRINGENT than OSHA 29 CFR 1926.

Title

Number

Mish 27

Revision

1

Approved By

Jeremy Thamert 03/01/2025

Name Date

Effective Date

3/1/2025

Page

1 of 3

1.0 PURPOSE

To define the requirements and responsibilities for control of construction equipment modifications, fabrications, and special purpose use in order to maintain safe operating conditions. Further, to ensure compliance with American National Standards Institute (ANSI), Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), and Washington Industrial Safety and Health Act (WISH) requirements, and the specifications of the manufacturer or registered Professional Engineer

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 The MH Construction Industrial Safety and Health Department (IS&H) is responsible for review and evaluation of proposed modifications. IS&H may require preparation of a formal Job Hazard Analysis (JHA) to include special operator training requirements.

2.2 The Key Supervisor shall be responsible for the use of equipment assigned to their areas. This responsibility includes the assignment of construction equipment to the crafts for use and for maintenance of that equipment

MH Construction Management shall be responsible for the design and/or review of modifications or fabrication of proposed construction equipment. This includes the preparation of design drawings, and alteration procedures, including the necessary nondestructive examinations as required.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Construction Equipment: Equipment used in the construction industry. This includes, but is not limited to, the following items:

  • Crane
  • Hoists
  • Light vehicles
  • Trucks
  • Earth-moving equipment
  • Tools and machines
  • Drilling Rigs

Equipment Modification: Alterations of construction equipment from the original configuration as received and/or designed by the equipment manufacturer.

Equipment Fabrication: Construction equipment designed and/or built on the jobsite. Equipment that is not manufactured but originated on the construction site may be original in design or have similarities to manufactured equipment.

Special Purpose or Nonstandard Use: Use of construction equipment for operations not originally considered by the equipment manufacturers.

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 MH Construction Project Management and MH Construction IS&H shall be notified prior to modifications or fabrications of equipment.

4.2 A thorough review of the equipment shall be performed by MH Construction Project Management and IS&H.

5.0 PROCEDURE

The process required to modify construction equipment is shown in attachment MISH 27-A.

5.1 Any request for modifications, fabrication or nonstandard use of construction equipment shall be made to MH Construction Project Management. All requests shall contain the following information as a minimum:

5.1.1 A description of the equipment, including information such as manufacturer’s model number, capacity, limitations on use, etc.

5.1.2 The reason for the request, including any information on availability of alternate equipment, schedule impact, cost effect, etc.

5.1.3 A description of the modifications, fabrications and/or special purpose use of construction equipment. Drawings or sketches of original equipment or configuration and the proposed alteration/fabrication shall be made available to MH Construction Project Management.

5.2 MH Construction Project Management shall review the request for modifications, fabrications, and/or nonstandard use in regard to the overall project need. This review includes consideration of the following:

5.2.1 Use of alternate equipment that may be available.

5.2.2 Direct purchase of special equipment to meet the desired needs.

5.2.3 Manufacturer’s specifications.

5.2.4 Safeness of the request.

IS&H will review the request for modification, fabrication and/or nonstandard use for compliance with the appropriate safety standards, and will consider special safety requirements imposed by IS&H, and either accepts or rejects the request. All requests for modification/fabrication shall be returned to MH Construction Project Management. Request, which have been rejected, shall include an explanation.

5.3 MH Construction Project Management shall review the design for the modification or fabrication based on the information received. An engineer will conduct this review. The design includes a review of the structural integrity of the modification/fabrication and any other review deemed appropriate.

5.4 Approved request shall be forwarded to IS&H for a final safety check along with any design drawings and procedure. A JHA may be required at this point. Upon final approval, the request is released to the Contractor for implementation and use.

5.5 Upon final approval, the MH Construction shall fabricate or modify equipment according to design.

6.0 RECORDS

Document

Record Submittal

Responsibility

Record Retention

Responsibility

Requests, Drawings, Approvals, etc.

Contractor

Project Management

7.0 REFERENCES

29 CRF 1910 Occupational Safety and Health Standards (OSHA)

29 CRF 1926 Safety and Health Regulations for Construction (OSHA)

WAC 296-155 Safety Standards for Construction Work (WISHA)

8.0 ATTACHMENTS

N/A
