Mh Construction Industrial Safety And Health Program

Title

Hand And Portable Power Tools

Number

Mish 36

Revision

1

Approved By

Jeremt Thamert 03/01/2025

Name Date

Effective Date

3/1/2025

Page

1 of 6

1.0 PURPOSE

To establish the requirements for the selection, use, and maintenance of hand and portable power-operated tools. Further, to ensure compliance with Occupational Safety and Health Administration/Washington Industrial Safety and Health Act (OSHA/WISHA) requirements.

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 The Key Supervisor shall be responsible for implementation and ensuring compliance with this procedure.

2.2 Employees shall be responsible for complying with the provisions of this procedure.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Not Applicable

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 The selection, use, and maintenance of hand and portable power-operated tools shall comply with the following statutory requirements:

  • 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Part 1926, Subpart I; Tools – Hand and Power.
  • 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Part 1910, Subpart P; Hand and Portable Powered Tools and Other Hand-held Equipment.
  • Washington Administrative Code (WAC) 296-155, Part G; Tools – Hand and Power.

4.2 Hand and power tools and similar equipment shall be maintained in safe condition.

4.3 Employees shall examine hand and power-operated tools prior to use. Defective tools shall be turned in for repair or replacement.

4.4 When power-operated tools are designed to accommodate blade/wheel guards, they shall be equipped with such guards when in use.

4.5 Appropriate personal protective equipment shall be identified, provided, and used for the safe operation of hand/power tools.

4.6 Power tools shall be disconnected from their energy source when changing attachments or conducting repairs/maintenance on the tool.

4.7 Requirements for personal protective equipment shall be identified and posted in shops and facilities, and wherever possible at construction sites. For stationary grinding machines, cut-off machines, etc., requirements shall be posted at the location of the machine.

4.8 Portable power tools shall be equipped with a constant-pressure switch that will shut off power when the operator releases the pressure.

EXCEPTIONS: Hand-held powered grinders with wheels two inches or less in diameter, routers, planers, laminate trimmers, nibblers, shears, scroll saws, and jig saws with blade shanks ¼ inches wide or less, may be equipped with a positive “ON-OFF” switch.

4.9 Blades and wheels shall have the proper rating and revolutions per minute for the tool.

5.0 PROCEDURE

5.1 HAND TOOLS

5.1.1 Wrenches with sprung jaws shall not be used.

5.1.2 Impact tools such as drift pins, wedges, and chisels shall be kept free of mushroomed heads.

5.1.3 Wooden handles of tools shall be kept free of splinters or cracks, and shall be kept tight in the tool.

5.2 ELECTRIC TOOLS

5.2.1 Electric power-operated tools shall be double insulated or grounded in accordance with MISH 29, and other applicable OSHA/WISHA/ANSI standards.

5.2.2 Power tools shall not be hoisted or lowered by their electric cords.

5.3 PNEUMATIC TOOLS

5.3.1 Pneumatic tools shall not be hoisted or lowered by their hoses.

5.3.2 Safety clips or retainers shall be securely installed and maintained on pneumatic impact tools to prevent attachments from being accidentally expelled.

5.3.3 All pneumatically driven nailers, staplers, and other similar equipment provided with an automatic fastener feed shall be equipped with a safety device on the muzzle to prevent the tools from ejecting fasteners when the muzzle is not in contact with the work surface.

EXCEPTION: Pneumatic nailers or staplers utilizing “fine wire” brads or staples do not require a muzzle contact safety device under the following circumstances:

  • The overall weight of the fastening device does not exceed the weight of 1-1/2 in. of standard 18 gauge wire.
  • The operator and any other person within 12 ft. of the point of operation wear approved eye protection.

5.3.4 All hoses exceeding ½ in. at the inside diameter shall have a safety device at the source of supply or branch line to reduce pressure in case of hose failure.

5.3.5 Pneumatic power tools shall be secured to the hose or whip to prevent the tool from becoming accidentally disconnected.

5.3.6 All pneumatic tools shall be equipped with deadman switches. Governor-controlled pneumatic tools shall be equipped with an air filter and oilier.

5.3.7 Pneumatic tools shall not be connected to an air supply, which exceeds the tool rating.

5.4 FUEL POWERED TOOLS

5.4.1 Fuel powered tools shall be stopped and allowed to cool prior to being refueled, serviced, or maintained.

5.5 HYDRAULIC POWERED TOOLS

5.5.1 Fluids used in hydraulic powered tools shall be fire-resistant fluids, approved under Schedule 30 of the Bureau of Mines.

5.5.2 The manufacturer’s safe operating pressures for hoses, valves, pipes, filters, and other fittings shall not be exceeded.

5.6 POWDER ACTUATED TOOLS

5.6.1 Only qualified operators (trained by the manufacturer) shall be permitted to use powder-actuated tools.

5.6.2 When in use, powder actuated tools shall be tested each day before loading (in accordance with the manufacturer’s recommended procedure) to ensure that safety devices are in proper working condition.

5.6.3 Tools shall not be loaded until just prior to the intended firing time. Neither loaded nor empty tools shall be pointed at persons.

5.6.4 Loaded tools shall not be left unattended.

5.6.5 Fasteners shall not be driven into very hard or brittle materials such as cast iron, glazed tile, hardened steel, glass block, live rock, face brink, or hollow tile.

5.6.6 Driving into easily penetrated materials shall be avoided, unless such materials are backed by a substance that will prevent the pin or fastener from passing completely through and creating a flying missile hazard on the other side.

5.6.7 Powder actuated tools shall not be use in atmospheres having a 10% or greater reading for Lower Explosive Limit.

5.6.8 Tools shall always be used with the correct shield, guard, or attachment as recommended by the manufacturer.

5.6.9 Powder actuated tools shall be stored in labeled, lockable containers so as to render them unavailable to unauthorized persons.

5.6.10 Tools shall be operated in strict accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions. Only those types of fasteners and powder loads recommended by the tool manufacturer shall be used.

5.6.11 Prior to driving a fastener, the operator shall inspect the line-of-fire to assure safety should the fastener penetrate completely through the work surface.

5.6.12 The tool shall be held perpendicular to the work surface when fastening into any material, except for specific applications recommended by the too manufacturer.

5.6.13 In the event of a misfire, the operator shall follow the explicit instructions set forth by the tool manufacturer.

5.6.14 A sign at least 8 x 10 inches, using boldface type at least 1 inch in height shall be posted in plain sight on all construction projects where powder actuated tools are in use. The sign shall bear wording similar to the following: “POWDER ACTUATED TOOL IN USE.”

5.6.15 Spent shell casing shall be picked-up and deposed daily.

5.6.16 Misfire shells shall be placed in water until the end of shift.

5.7 ABRASIVE WHEELS AND TOOLS

5.7.1 Abrasive wheels shall be closely inspected and ring-tested before mounting to ensure that they are free from cracks or defects.

5.7.2 Training shall be provided for those employees who may be required to perform ring-testing; ring-testing is conducted only by trained personnel.

5.7.3 Machine spindle speeds shall be checked before mounting the wheel to be certain that the wheel will not exceed the maximum operating speed marked on the wheel.

5.7.4 Abrasive wheels shall be used only on machines with safety guards except as follows:

  • Wheels used for internal work while within the work being ground.
  • Mounted wheels 2 in. and smaller in diameter used in portable operations.
  • Types 16, 17, 18, 18R, and 19 cones and plugs, and threaded hole pot balls where the work offers protection.

5.7.5 Abrasive wheels shall be handled and stored in a manner that prevents damage to the wheels.

5.7.6 Stationary grinding machines shall be equipped with a transparent spark shield, tongue guards (adjusted within ¼ inch of the wheel), and a work rest (adjusted within 1/8 inch of the wheel).

5.7.7 Stationary grinding machines (impermanent locations/shops) used for dry grinding shall have provisions to exhaust or control dusts.

5.7.8 Pneumatic grinding machines shall be operated at the correct pressures and off a regulated air supply.

5.8 WOODWORKING TOOLS: All portable power-driven circular saws shall be equipped with guards above and below the base plate or shoe. Lower guards shall be properly maintained to ensure that they will automatically and instantly return to the covering position when the tool is withdrawn from the work.

5.9 JACKS – LEVER AND RATCHET, SCREW, AND HYDRAULIC

5.9.1 The manufacturer’s rated capacity shall be legibly marked on all jacks, and shall not be exceeded.

5.9.2 All jacks shall be provided with a positive stop to prevent over-travel.

5.9.3 When it is necessary to provide a firm foundation, the base of the jack shall be blocked or cribbed. When there is a possibility of slippage of the metal cap of the jack, a wood block shall be placed between the cap and the load.

5.9.4 After a load has been raised, it shall be immediately cribbed, blocked, or otherwise secured.

5.9.5 Jacks shall be properly lubricated at regular intervals, in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions.

6.0 RECORDS

Not Applicable

7.0 REFERENCES

American National Standards Institute (ANSI) A10.3-1985; Safety Requirement for Powder Actuated Fastening Systems.

ANSI B7.1-1988, the Use, care, and Protection of Abrasive Wheels

ANSI 01.1-1961, Safety Code for Woodworking Machinery

8.0 ATTACHMENTS

Not Applicable

Training & Reference Resources

Travelers Training Library: https://www.travelers.com/risk-control/safety-education-training

OSHA Reference: https://www.osha.gov/construction

OSHA Reference: https://www.osha.gov/laws-regs/regulations/standardnumber/1926

1.0 PURPOSE

To establish written policies regarding how MH Construction and its employees handle project waste, trash, and/ or scrap materials.

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 The Key Supervisor shall ensure compliance with this procedure.

2.2 Employees are responsible for complying with this procedure.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Not Applicable

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 Before any project begins an assessment will be conducted to determine how much waste, trash and or scrap materials will be produced. To minimize the impact on the environment all materials shall be sorted accordingly.

4.2 All employees on the project shall be informed and educated with as to the proper disposal method for all waste, trash, and scrap materials produced on the work site.

4.3 Waste materials will be disposed in a method of minimal environmental impact such as using the proper tie down methods when transporting waste materials.

4.4 Trash shall be disposed in an environmentally safe process and will be taken disposed in the correct waste receptacle on site.

4.5 All materials that can be recycled and or used on other job sites, employees shall separate all materials from other scrap materials and brought back to the main office and put in the proper storage.

5.0 PROCEDURE

Not Applicable

6.0 RECORDS

Not Applicable

Not Applicable

Number

Revision

1

1 of 3

1.0 PURPOSE

To establish requirements for concrete and masonry construction, and to ensure compliance with Occupational Safety and Health Administration/Washington Industrial Safety and Health Act (OSHA/WISHA) requirements.

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 The Key Supervisor shall be responsible for implementation and ensuring compliance with this procedure.

2.2 Employees shall be responsible for complying with the provisions of this procedure.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Not Applicable

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 Concrete and masonry construction work shall be conducted in accordance with the following statutory requirements:

  • 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Part 1926, Subpart Q; Concrete and Masonry Construction.
  • Washington Administrative Code (WAC) 296-155, Part O; Concrete, Concrete Forms, Shoring and Masonry Construction.

4.2 Lift-slab operations shall be in accordance with the requirements of ANSI A10.9 sections 11 and 29, and CFR 1926.305(a) and (b).

4.3 Scaffolds shall be constructed, used, and maintained in accordance with the provisions of MISH 22.

4.4 Construction loads shall not be placed on a concrete structure or portion of a concrete structure unless it has been determined that the structure or portion o f the structure is capable of supporting the loads. Such a determination shall be based on information received from a person qualified in structural design.

4.5 Protruding reinforcing steel, onto and into which employees could fall, shall be guarded to eliminate the hazard of impalement.

4.6 Employees shall not be permitted to ride or work under concrete buckets.

4.7 Hand protection shall be worn by employees applying and finishing cement, sand, and water mixtures.

4.8 Employees shall not be permitted to place or tie reinforcing steel more than 6 ft. above any adjacent working surface unless they have appropriate fall protection in accordance with the provisions of MISH 11.

4.9 When service or maintenance of machinery may expose employees to the unexpected release or activation of hazardous energy, such work shall be conducted in accordance with the provisions of MISH 15.

4.10 Masonry saws shall be guarded with a semicircular enclosure over the blade.

5.0 PROCEDURE

5.1 EQUIPMENT AND TOOLS

5.1.1 Manually guided, powered, and rotating concrete troweling machines shall be equipped with a control switch that automatically shuts off the power whenever the hands of the operator are removed from the equipment handles.

5.1.2 Concrete buggy handles shall not extend beyond the wheels on either side of the buggy.

5.1.3 Compressed air hoses used on concrete pumping systems shall be provided with positive fail-safe joint connectors to prevent the separation of sections when the hose is pressurized.

5.1.4 Concrete buckets equipped with hydraulic or pneumatic gates shall have positive safety latches or equivalent safety devices to prevent premature or accidental dumping.

5.2 FORMS AND SHORING

5.2.1 Form work shall be designed, fabricated, erected, supported, braced, and maintained to support, without failure, all vertical and lateral loads that may be reasonably anticipated to be applied to the form work, and in accordance with MISH 26.

5.2.2 Shoring equipment shall be inspected prior to erection to determine that the equipment is in safe condition. Shoring equipment that is found not to be in safe condition shall not be used.

5.2.3 Forms and shores shall not be removed until it has been determined that the concrete has gained sufficient strength to support its weight and superimposed loads.

5.3 MASONRY: Whenever a masonry wall is constructed, a limited access zone shall be established as follows:

  • The limited access zone is established prior to the start of construction of the wall.
  • The zone is equal to the height of the wall to be constructed – plus 4 ft. – and runs the entire length of the wall.
  • The zone is established on the side of the wall where the scaffolding will be removed.
  • The zone is restricted. Employees are not permitted to enter the zone unless they are actively engaged in constructing the wall.
  • The zone remains in place until the wall is adequately supported to prevent it from overturning or collapsing. If the wall is over 8 ft. high, the zone remains in place until permanent supporting elements of the structure are in place.

6.0 RECORDS

Not Applicable

7.0 REFERENCES

American National Standards Institute (ANSI) A10.9, Safety Requirements for Concrete Construction and Masonry Work

8.0 ATTACHMENTS

Not Applicable

Mh Construction Industrial Safety And Health Program

Title

Miscellaneous Construction Requirements

Number

Mish 31

Revision

1

Approved By

Jeremy Thamert 03/01/2025

Name Date

Effective Date

3/1/2025

Page

1 of 5

1.0 PURPOSE

To define responsibilities and safety requirements for roofing, insulating and waterproofing, rock crushing, gravel washing, hot mix plants, moving structures and to ensure compliance with Occupational Safety and Health Administration/Washington Industrial Safety and Health Act (OSHA/WISHA) requirements.

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 The Key Supervisor shall be responsible for the following:

2.1.1 Monitoring those operations involving roofing, insulating and waterproofing, rock crushing, gravel washing, hot mix plants, and moving structures.

2.1.2 To ensure compliance to this procedure and applicable OSHA/WISHA requirements.

2.1.3 To ensure all pre-job planning documents (MISH 5) are completed, submitted and approved prior to the start of work.

2.2 Employees shall be responsible for complying with the provisions of this procedure.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Not Applicable

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 ROOFING, INSULATING AND WATERPROOFING

4.1.1 Roofer hoisting jacks shall be constructed to withstand the contemplated load to be hoisted. The beam from counter balance point to the heel of jack shall be ¾ the length of the entire beam.

4.1.2 A steel collar or U-bolt and shackle on the head of the hoisting jack shall be provided for attachment of pulley.

4.1.3 Hoisting pulleys shall be of steel construction.

4.1.4 When hoisting is done by hand, the hoist line shall be not less than five-eighths manila rope or equivalent. Where a machine hoist is used, the hoist line shall be wire rope.

4.1.5 Workers shall not stand under a load when material or hot asphalt is being hoisted.

4.1.6 Hot asphalt shall be kept at a safe level in buckets for carrying and hoisting.

4.1.7 There shall be means of smothering fires from fired tar pots.

4.1.8 Persons working at kettles or handling hot tar shall wear gloves and have arms fully protected.

4.1.9 Tar pots shall have an attendant at all times while in operation.

4.2 ROCK CRUSHING, GRAVEL WASHING, AND HOT MIX PLANTS

4.2.1 Stationary dragline machines shall have all moving parts, which are exposed to contact, guarded with standard safeguards.

4.2.2 When dumping materials from trucks, truck dump bunkers shall have wheel bumper blocks installed.

4.2.3 Crusher operators and other employees working where hazardous dust or nuisance dust exist shall use approved respirators and goggles.

4.2.4 Dusty rock crushing houses or other dusty places of employment, shall be equipped with means for controlling the dust.

4.2.5 Aggregate elevators, bucket or other type, shall have guards or barricades installed under or around the return strand, and be of sufficient strength to sustain the weight of piled up broken elevator equipment.

4.2.6 Overhead conveyors shall be constructed so as to restrain the spillage of material. Wherever the hazard of falling materials exists, overhead protection shall be provided over walkways and roadways.

4.2.7 Mixer operator shall use approved respirators and goggles, except when operating from remote locations

4.2.8 Dust and fume collection systems shall be provided on all installations. Dust and fumes shall be discharged back into the plant or carried to a suitable distance from the work area and precipitated.

4.3 MOVING OF STRUCTURES

4.3.1 Cribbing and blocking shall be set on level and firm foundations.

4.3.2 Trailers in temporary storage shall be secured in such a manner that winds in excess of 60 mph cannot dislodge trailers off temporary blocks.

4.3.3 Dollies and rollers shall be securely blocked except when the structure is being moved by power equipment.

4.3.4 Jacks shall comply with applicable OSHA/WISHA standards.

4.3.5 When a structure is being lifted, shoring shall be provided at all times and be kept up to the object until the desired height is reached, and then the structure shall be blocked or cribbed immediately.

4.4 STEEL ERECTION

4.4.1 Steel erection shall be conducted in accordance with the following statutory requirements:

  • Code of Federal Regulations, title 29, Part 1926 (29 CFR 1926), Safety and Health Regulations for construction, Subpart R, “Steel Erection.”
  • Washington Administrative Code, Title 296, Chapter 155 (WAC 296-155), Safety Standards for Construction Work, PRT p, “steel Erection.”

4.4.2 The selection, use, and maintenance of fall protection equipment and personal protective equipment shall be in accordance with MISH 10 and MISH 11, and the statutory requirements referenced therein.

4.4.3 Crane booms, drill rig masts, etc. 100 ft. or more in height above ground level shall be provided with an operable lighting system to identify their location to aircraft during hours of darkness. Such obstruction lighting shall consist of flashing white strobe lights which shall function from dusk to daylight as a minimum

4.5 STRUCTURAL STEEL ASSEMBLY

4.5.1 During the final placing of solid web structural members, the load shall not be released from the hoisting line until the members are secured with not less than two bolts, or the equivalent, at each connection and drawn up wrench tight.

4.5.2 Open web steel joists shall not be placed on any structural steel framework unless such framework is safety bolted or welded.

4.5.3 In steel framing, where bar joists are utilized and columns are not framed in at least two directions with structural steel members, bar joists shall be field-bolted at columns to provide lateral stability during construction.

4.5.4 Where long-span joists or trusses 40 ft. or longer are used, a center row of bolted bridging shall be installed to provide lateral stability during construction prior to slacking of the hoisting line.

4.5.5 Tag lines shall be used for controlling loads.

4.6 BOLTING, RIVETING, FITTING-UP AND PLUMBING-UP

4.6.1 Containers shall be provided for storing or carrying rivets, bolts, and drift pins. Such containers shall be secured against accidental displacement when aloft.

4.6.2 Pneumatic hand tools shall be disconnected from the power source and pressure in hose lines is released before any adjustments or repairs are made.

4.6.3 When bolts or drift pins are being knocked out, means shall be provided to keep them from falling.

4.6.4 Impact wrenches shall be provided with a locking device for retaining the socket.

4.6.5 Turnbuckles shall be secured to prevent unwinding while under stress.

4.6.6 Plumbing-up guys shall be removed only under the supervision of a competent person.

4.7 FLOORING

4.7.1 Derrick and erection floors shall be solidly planked or decked over their entire surface except for access openings. Planking shall not be less than 2 in. thick, full size, undressed and is laid tight and secured to prevent movement.

4.7.2 On buildings or structures not adaptable to temporary floors and where scaffolds are not used, safety nets or other equivalent means of fall protection shall be provided and maintained whenever the potential fall distance exceeds 10 ft.

4.7.3 Safety railings and midrails of ½ in. wire rope or the equivalent shall be installed around the periphery of all temporary-planked or temporary metal-decked floors of tier buildings and other multi-floored structures during structural steel assembly.

4.7.4 Where skeleton steel erection is being done, a tightly planked and substantial floor shall be maintained within two stories or 25 ft. (whichever is less) below and directly under that portion of each tier of beams on which any work is being performed. (Where such a floor is not practicable, subsection 4.6 applies.)

4.8 CORE DRILLING/CONCRETE SAW CUTTING OPERATIONS

4.8.1 Where electrical systems may be contacted, these systems shall be locked and tagged out prior to beginning the operation (in accordance with MISH 15).

4.8.2 Personal protective equipment (see MISH 29, section 5.2) shall be specified on the JHA.

4.8.3 Pneumatic powered drilling equipment shall use safety chains or other equivalent safety/locking devices at all hose connections and couplings.

4.8.4 When core drill locations are laid out and the core drill is expected to penetrate within 16 in. of an exposed conduit or pipe, a metal plate (approximately 1/8 in. thick) shall be placed between the conduit/pipe and the proposed area of core drilling.

4.8.5 The operator shall approach surface “breakthrough” cautiously to minimize and control penetration of the drill.

5.0 PROCEDURE

Not Applicable.

.

6.0 RECORDS

Not Applicable

7.0 REFERENCES

MISH 5 Prejob Safety planning

29 CRF 1926 Safety and Health Standards for Construction (OSHA)

WAC 296-155 Safety Regulations for Construction Work (WISHA), Part R

8.0 ATTACHMENTS

Not Applicable

Mh Construction Industrial Safety And Health Program

Title

Subcontractor Management Program

Number

Mish 39

Revision

1

Approved By

Jeremy Thamert 03/01/2025

Name Date

Effective Date

3/1/2025

Page

1 of 5

1.0 PURPOSE

To ensure that MH Construction, Inc continues to improve the subcontractors health, safety and environmental performance and to establish a standards for pre-qualification, evaluation/selection and development of our subcontractors. This implies to all subcontractors and all MH Construction Inc. locations.

2.0 SCOPE

This implies to all subcontractors and all MH Construction Inc. Locations.

3.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

3.1 All MH Construction Inc. subcontractors are to be managed in accordance with this program.

3.2 The use of subcontractors must be pre-approved by MH Construction. Approval requires as follows:

3.2.1 A formal safety review will be performed by MH Construction, which will include a review of the subcontractors safety program, safety training documentation and safety statistics.

3.2.2 The scope of the review will include the hazards and risk exposures.

3.2.3 All subcontractors must agree to abide by MH Constructions Drug and Alcohol Policy and Onsite Safety Rules throughout the duration of the work being preformed.

3.2.4 All subcontractors will be informed on the safety policies, requirements, and expectations MH Construction requires.

3.2.5 Any subcontractor that has a “NON-Approved” safety status will not be allowed on any MH Construction site until further notice.

4.0 PROCEDURE

4.1 Pre-Qualifications for subcontractors as follow:

4.1.1 Subcontractors will qualify when safety programs, safety training documents and safety statistics are reviews and meet the required standards.

4.2 Evaluation Safety Metrics will also be used for pre-qualification and selecting subcontractors. The safety metrics and scoring will need to meet the following to be considered:

4.2.1 MH Construction Subcontractor Safety Pre-Qualification Form response and subcontractor safety program documents review with a 60%( rated from 0-60points)

5.0 EVALUATION RATING AND ACCEPTANCE

5.1 The subcontractors rating system has five designations, as follows:

5.1.1 A – No restrictions. (Equals to or greater than 90 Points)

5.1.2 B – Mitigation plan must be documented & approved by MH Construction safety. (Between 80 and 89 Points)

5.1.3 C- Mitigation plan must be documented and approved by MH Construction safety and management approval in written agreement. (Between 71 and 79 points)

5.1.4 D- Mandatory commitment meeting with senior subcontractor management present, mitigation plan documented, and approved by MH Construction safety personnel; management approval in written agreement; Trained subcontractor safety personnel on site while any work is being performed under any circumstance of the number of employees on site. (Between 61 and 70 points)

5.1.5 F- Will not be considered. (Less than 60 points)

5.2 All subcontractors will be evaluated and scored by MH Construction Personnel. MH Construction Personnel will then provide Management with the scores and rankings of all subcontractors for acceptance.

5.3 MH Construction Inc. reserves the right to change a subcontractors state to “Non-Approved” if the subcontractor shows any insufficient progress towards any mitigations plan and or otherwise agreed upon criteria.

6.0 SUBCONTACTOR INVOLVEMENT

6.1 Subcontractors are required to follow and implement the work practices and systems described below, while performing work at MH Construction worksites:

6.1.1 Attend mandatory safety orientation, pre-job meeting or kick-off meeting provided by MH Construction prior to any work being performed.

6.1.2 Monitor employees for substance abuse or report non-conformities to MH Construction.

6.1.3 Ensure personnel has the required training and competency for their work.

6.1.4 Participate in MH Construction Tool Box Safety Meetings, job safety analysis, and or hazard assessments on job safety inspections.

6.1.5 Preform a pre-job safety inspections which includes an equipment inspection as well.

6.1.6 Participate in the BBS hazard reporting system.

6.1.7 Report all injuries, spills, property damages, incidents, and near misses.

6.1.8 Comply with onsite and owner client safety rules.

6.1.9 Implement MH Construction safety practices and processes as applicable.

6.1.10 Clean up and restore worksite the work performed is finished.

6.1.11 Ensure compliance with regulations at all times.

6.1.12 Post job safety performance reviews shall be conducted for subcontractors.

6.0 RECORDS

Not Applicable

7.0 REFERENCES

Not Applicable

8.0 ATTACHMENTS

Not Applicable

TRAVELERS AUDIT FINDING 26-2 — ACTION REQUIRED

MHC subcontracts were missing best practice risk transfer elements as of the

April 8, 2026 Travelers Risk Control consultation.

Specifically: CGL/Umbrella coverage with completed operations and Additional

Insured status through the statute of repose was not required.

This section establishes the MHC contractual risk transfer standard.

1.0 PURPOSE

1.1 Effective contractual risk transfer protects MH Construction from liability resulting from subcontractor operations and requires subcontractors to take financial responsibility for their own exposures.

1.2 All MHC subcontracts must include the insurance requirements defined in this section. Review with your insurance agent (Hub International Northwest) and legal counsel before finalizing any subcontract.

2.0 REQUIRED SUBCONTRACTOR INSURANCE

Coverage Type

Minimum Limit

Additional Insured Required?

Duration

Commercial General Liability (CGL)

$1,000,000 per occurrence / $2,000,000 aggregate

Yes — MHC as AI

Project + statute of repose

Commercial Umbrella / Excess Liability

$1,000,000 minimum

Yes — MHC as AI

Project + statute of repose

Workers Compensation

Statutory limits (WA)

N/A

Duration of work

Employers Liability

$500,000 each accident

N/A

Duration of work

Commercial Auto Liability

$1,000,000 combined single limit

Yes — MHC as AI

Duration of work

Completed Operations Coverage

Included in CGL/Umbrella

Yes — MHC as AI

Statute of repose (WA = 6 years)

3.0 CONTRACT REQUIREMENTS

3.1 All subcontracts must require subcontractors to name MH Construction, Inc. as an Additional Insured on their CGL, Umbrella, and Auto policies.

3.2 Certificates of Insurance (COI) must be obtained from all subcontractors before work begins. COIs must be reviewed by the Safety Officer and retained in the project file.

3.3 Subcontractors must provide 30 days written notice to MHC prior to cancellation or material change of any required insurance coverage.

3.4 Completed operations coverage must remain in force for the duration of the Washington State statute of repose (6 years from substantial completion).

3.5 Review subcontract insurance requirements with Hub International Northwest LLC (Justin Hudon) and legal counsel at least annually.

4.0 INDEMNIFICATION

4.1 All subcontracts must include a broad-form indemnification clause requiring the subcontractor to defend, indemnify, and hold harmless MH Construction from claims arising from the subcontractor's work.

4.2 Indemnification language must be reviewed by legal counsel to ensure compliance with Washington State anti-indemnity statutes (RCW 4.24.115).

1.0 PURPOSE

To assist the Contractor in identifying, responding to, and reporting the various types of emergencies that may be encountered. Furthermore, to ensure compliance with Occupational Safety and Health Administration/Washington Industrial Safety and health Act (OSHA/WISHA) requirements.

2.0 RESPONSIBILITY

2.1 Project Management is responsible for interacting with operating contractors to assist them in identifying additional emergency response signals and procedures that may be required in specific buildings or work areas.

2.2 The Key Supervisor shall be responsible for the following:

2.2.1 Ensuring that each employee is knowledgeable of the emergency response and notification requirements in the areas where contractor personnel are working.

2.2.2 Accounting for all contractor employees under emergency conditions.

2.3 Employees shall be responsible for the following:

2.3.1 Responding to all emergency signals and following directions.

2.3.2 Promptly proceeding to the staging area and reporting to their supervisor when required by the nature of the emergency.

3.0 DEFINITIONS

Not Applicable

4.0 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

4.1 For purposes of this document, the following shall be considered as types of emergencies that may be encountered:

 Personal (accident/injury)

 Fire

 Earthquake

 Weather (severe cold, rain, snow, wind, etc.)

 Hazardous material exposure

 Cave-ins

 Sabotage

4.2 To notify MH Construction, the Contractor shall call MH Construction, Construction Management 308-6489.

4.3 Contractor personnel at remote worksites shall have communication equipment to initiate effective emergency response and be notified of emergency situations.

5.0 PROCEDURE

5.1 During the employee orientation (refer to MISH 4, Safety and Health Orientation), the Key Supervisor shall orient employees to the emergency response and notification requirements.

5.2 Because of the number of facilities involved, specific information regarding staging areas, additional emergency response and notification requirements that may be applicable to some buildings or operations have not been provided. Contact the MH Construction Project Management for specific information regarding requirements for each facility.

5.3 For all work locations, the key supervisor shall coordinate with the owner of the facility to identify staging areas, and emergency signal. If staging areas have not been identified the key supervisor shall identify the staging area and emergency signals for the specific job site.

6.0 RECORDS

Not applicable

29 CFR 1926 Safety and Health Regulations for Construction (OSHA)

WAC 296-155 Safety Standards for Construction Work (WISHA)

MISH 49 — Incident Investigation & Root Cause Analysis

Chapter 8: Emergency Response & Incident Management | 29 CFR 1926.20 | AGC: Incident Investigation

DOCUMENT

REVISION

APPROVED BY

3.0

April 2026

Jeremy Thamert

President

Safety Officer

Travelers Risk Control Consultant

AGC Representative

Jeremy Thamert

Matt Ramsey

_______________________

_______________________

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

Signature / Date

1.0 PURPOSE

1.1 All incidents, near-misses, and property damage events must be investigated to identify root causes and prevent recurrence.

1.2 Investigation is not about assigning blame — it is about identifying system failures and implementing corrective actions.

2.0 INVESTIGATION REQUIREMENTS

2.1 Immediate Response (0–4 Hours)

  • Secure the scene. Prevent further injury.
  • Provide first aid / call 911 if required.
  • Notify Safety Officer and Project Manager immediately.
  • Preserve physical evidence — do not disturb the scene until documented.
  • Conduct drug/alcohol testing per MISH 06 if required.
  • Notify Travelers Risk Control if incident may result in a claim.

2.2 Investigation (Within 24 Hours)

  • Photograph the scene from multiple angles.
  • Interview all witnesses separately. Document statements.
  • Collect physical evidence (equipment, materials, PPE).
  • Review training records, inspection logs, and work procedures.
  • Complete the MHC Incident Investigation Report (Form 49-A).

2.3 Root Cause Analysis

Use the 5-Why method to identify root causes beyond the immediate cause:

  • Why 1: Why did the incident occur? (Immediate cause)
  • Why 2: Why did that condition/action exist? (Contributing cause)
  • Why 3: Why was the contributing cause not prevented? (System failure)
  • Why 4: Why did the system failure occur? (Root cause)
  • Why 5: Why was the root cause not identified earlier? (Program gap)

2.4 Corrective Actions

2.4.1 All root causes must have a corresponding corrective action with a responsible party and a completion deadline.

2.4.2 Corrective actions must be verified as complete by the Safety Officer before the investigation is closed.

2.4.3 Lessons learned must be communicated to all affected crews at the next safety meeting.

3.0 RECORDKEEPING

3.1 All incident investigations must be documented on Form 49-A and retained for a minimum of 5 years.

3.2 OSHA 300/300A logs must be maintained and posted per 29 CFR 1904.

3.3 Travelers Risk Control must be notified of any incident that may result in a claim.

1.0 PURPOSE

1.1 MH Construction is committed to returning injured employees to productive work as quickly and safely as possible.

1.2 A formal Return-to-Work (RTW) program reduces workers compensation costs, maintains employee morale, and demonstrates MHC's commitment to its workforce.

2.0 PROGRAM ELEMENTS

2.1 Modified Duty

2.1.1 MHC will make reasonable efforts to provide modified or light duty assignments for injured employees who are medically cleared to return with restrictions.

2.1.2 Modified duty assignments must be within the employee's medical restrictions and must be documented by the treating physician.

2.1.3 Modified duty is temporary. The goal is full return to the employee's regular position.

2.2 Medical Management

2.2.1 The Safety Officer will maintain contact with the injured employee and treating physician throughout the recovery process.

2.2.2 MHC will designate a preferred medical provider for occupational injuries. Employees must use the designated provider for initial treatment.

2.2.3 All medical restrictions must be communicated to the Project Manager and Safety Officer before the employee returns to any work activity.

2.3 Return-to-Work Agreement

2.3.1 Before returning to work after a lost-time injury, the employee must sign Form 50-A (Return-to-Work Agreement) acknowledging their restrictions and modified duty assignment.

3.0 WORKERS COMPENSATION

3.1 All work-related injuries must be reported to the Safety Officer immediately and to Washington State L&I within the required timeframe.

3.2 MHC will cooperate fully with L&I and Travelers in the management of all workers compensation claims.
